title: Build concolic orchestrator (single-function exploration loop)
description: Implement the core concolic loop in shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs: (1) generate initial inputs, (2) execute function via frontend, (3) collect branch decisions and symbolic constraints, (4) negate uncovered branch constraints, (5) solve for new inputs via Z3, (6) repeat until coverage plateau or budget exhausted. Uses a worklist of branch targets ordered by novelty. This is the heart of shatter's exploration engine.

title: SymExpr builder parity properties (buildSymExpr vs buildSymExprWithFlow)
description:
The str-ho1d issue explicitly requested parity testing between buildSymExpr() and buildSymExprWithFlow() in shatter-ts instrumentor.ts. This was not delivered — only structural validity tests landed.

Needed: for arbitrary AST-like inputs that both functions handle, verify they produce compatible SymExpr outputs. When buildSymExprWithFlow returns a non-unknown result, buildSymExpr should handle the same node type (and vice versa). This is the primary source of silent concolic degradation — if one function returns unknown for a node the other handles, variables become invisible to the solver.

Approach: generate synthetic AST nodes (BinaryExpression, PropertyAccessExpression, CallExpression, etc.) and run both builders, asserting that neither returns unknown when the other returns a concrete SymExpr.

title: Deep proptest coverage for solver bridge (solve_for_new_path, to_z3_expr, overlay pipeline)
description:
The solver bridge is the most bug-prone code in shatter-core (str-6ayh class: wrong Z3 sorts for params). Current PBT has only 5 shallow infer_sort checks. Needed:

1. solve_for_new_path: arbitrary constraint sets with known-type params → solved values match ParamInfo types. Especially: string params never get Int solutions, nullable params get null or inner-type solutions.
2. to_z3_expr: arbitrary well-typed SymExpr trees → Z3 context accepts without sort mismatch errors. Requires spinning up Z3 context in test.
3. Full solve→overlay pipeline: arbitrary (constraints, base_inputs, param_names) triples → overlay output types match input types, length preserved, solved indices are valid.
4. Negation index properties: for any valid constraint list, negate at each index → either solves or returns unsat (never panics, never produces wrong-type values).
5. String theory integration: arbitrary string constraints (indexOf, contains, startsWith) → solutions are valid strings (not integers).

This is high-value PBT — these are the exact invariants that have caused real bugs.

title: Rust executor generates duplicate main() when source file contains fn main()
description:
## Bug

After fixing the stale binary issue (str-ss79), Rust function execution still fails with a compilation error:
```
error[E0428]: the name `main` is defined multiple times
```

## Root Cause

`generate_harness()` in `shatter-rust/src/executor.rs:173-177` concatenates:
1. The entire instrumented source (which includes `fn main()` from the original file)
2. Its own `fn main() {` harness wrapper

This produces a `main.rs` with two `main()` definitions.

Example source files affected:
- `examples/standalone/rust/01_arithmetic.rs` — has `fn main() { ... }`
- `examples/standalone/rust/02_strings.rs` — likely same
- `examples/standalone/rust/04_errors.rs` — likely same

## Reproduction

```bash
cargo build --manifest-path shatter-rust/Cargo.toml
echo '{...handshake...}
{...instrument file=examples/standalone/rust/01_arithmetic.rs...}
{...execute function=classify_number inputs=[42]...}' | ./shatter-rust/target/debug/shatter-rust
# → compilation_error: the name `main` is defined multiple times
```

## Fix Options

1. **Strip main()**: Before embedding, remove or rename `fn main()` from the instrumented source. Could use syn to parse and filter, or a regex to strip it.
2. **Module wrapping**: Include the instrumented source as a module (`mod user_code { ... }`) and call the target function via `user_code::function_name()`. This avoids name collisions entirely.
3. **Attribute gating**: Add `#[cfg(not(shatter_harness))]` around the original main and compile with `--cfg shatter_harness`.

Option 2 (module wrapping) is the cleanest — it also handles other potential name collisions (not just main).

title: Walkthrough steps 10-11 fail: shatter-rust binary not rebuilt after str-rv0k fix
description:
## Bug

Walkthrough steps 10 (Explore Rust Functions) and 11 (Scan Rust Examples) fail with:
```
execute error (InvalidRequest): execute command requires a file path
```

## Root Cause

Commit 09bba12 (fix for str-rv0k) added last_file fallback to shatter-rust handler.rs, but the binary at `shatter-rust/target/debug/shatter-rust` was never rebuilt. The CLI picks up the stale binary which still has the old code without the fallback.

Verified by:
- `strings shatter-rust/target/debug/shatter-rust | grep 'execute command requires'` shows the short error message (pre-fix)
- Binary timestamp is older than the source commit timestamp
- After `cargo build --manifest-path shatter-rust/Cargo.toml`, the fallback works correctly

## Fix

Add `cargo build --manifest-path shatter-rust/Cargo.toml` to the walkthrough preamble (before Rust steps). A no-op build takes ~0.2s so there's no meaningful cost when the binary is already current. This prevents stale-binary class of bugs permanently.

title: Telemetry first-run notice (opt-out with disclosure)
description: On first invocation when no telemetry config file exists at ~/.config/shatter/telemetry.yaml: Print to stderr (one time only): 'Shatter collects anonymous usage data to improve the tool. No file paths, source code, or personal information is collected. To disable: shatter telemetry off (or set SHATTER_TELEMETRY=off)'. Create config file with enabled: true, first_notice_shown: true. Runs before command dispatch, after consent check. If SHATTER_TELEMETRY=off or DO_NOT_TRACK=1 is already set, skip notice entirely and don't create config file.

title: shatter telemetry CLI subcommand
description: Add CliCommand::Telemetry variant with sub-subcommands: status (print consent state, config file location, anonymous ID truncated, queue file location + event count), off (write enabled: false to ~/.config/shatter/telemetry.yaml), on (write enabled: true), reset-id (regenerate anonymous ID and salt). Update demo/walkthrough.sh to exercise 'shatter telemetry status'. Per project rules: never add a CLI command without updating the walkthrough.

title: CLI bad-args capture via try_parse_from
description: In shatter-cli/src/main.rs, change Cli::parse() (line 2874) to Cli::try_parse_from(std::env::args_os()). On Err(clap_err): if telemetry enabled, sanitize args with path enrichment metadata, queue bad_cli_args event, then call clap_err.exit(). Map clap::error::ErrorKind to error_kind field: UnknownArgument→unknown_flag, InvalidSubcommand→unknown_subcommand, EmptyValue→missing_value, MissingRequiredArgument→missing_required, ValueValidation→invalid_value, WrongNumberOfValues→wrong_count, Other→other. Must not delay error output — queue is a fast local append. Sanitized args include path_meta probes (exists, kind, parent_exists, parent_writable, depth) for path-like values.

title: Epic: Telemetry — Anonymous Usage & Bad-Args Reporting
description: Privacy-preserving telemetry system for Shatter CLI. Captures bad CLI arguments (what features users expect but don't find), general anonymized usage metrics, and path-error diagnostics. Opt-out with first-run notice, local JSONL queue, no network in Phase 1. PostHog integration deferred to Phase 2. Full design: docs/plans/telemetry-anonymous-usage-bad-args.md
notes: Epic for issues: str-1k1g (core module), str-iw38 (bad-args capture), str-jhks (command events), str-wxf9 (telemetry subcommand), str-2usm (first-run notice), str-rtyl (PostHog Phase 2). Plan: docs/plans/telemetry-anonymous-usage-bad-args.md

title: Rust frontend execute handler requires file field not sent by core, breaking Rust scan
description:
## Bug

Scanning Rust examples fails with:
```
execute error (InvalidRequest): execute command requires a file path
```
for every function (8/8 skipped).

## Root Cause

The core's `Command::Execute` (protocol.rs:68-78) does NOT include a `file` field — it only sends `function`, `inputs`, `mocks`, and optional `setup_context`. The protocol design assumes the frontend remembers the file from the prior Analyze/Instrument step.

The Rust frontend's `handle_execute()` (handler.rs:303-312) requires `req.file` to be `Some` and returns an InvalidRequest error when it's `None`. Since the core never sends `file` in Execute, this always fails.

The TS frontend handles this correctly: it stores `lastAnalyzedFile` from the Analyze/Instrument steps and uses it as a fallback in `resolveFileForExecute()` (handlers.ts:346-358).

## Fix

The Rust frontend should store the file path from Analyze and Instrument requests in handler state (e.g., `self.last_file: Option<String>`), then use that as the fallback in `handle_execute()` when `req.file` is absent. This matches the TS frontend's approach.

title: Go frontend omits constraint field in BranchDecision, causing Rust deserialization failure
description:
## Bug

Running Go examples fails with:
```
[error] Exploration error for ClassifyNumber: frontend error: failed to deserialize response: missing field `constraint` at line 1 column 1785
```

## Root Cause

Go's `BranchDecision` struct in `protocol/types.go:170` has:
```go
Constraint *SymConstraint `json:"constraint,omitempty"`
```

When no symbolic constraint exists (the common case — Go doesn't do symbolic analysis), `Constraint` is `nil`, and `omitempty` causes Go to omit the field from JSON entirely.

Rust's `BranchDecision` in `execution_record.rs:36` has:
```rust
pub constraint: SymConstraint,  // required, no Option, no serde(default)
```

So Rust's serde rejects the response as missing a required field.

## Fix

Two-sided fix for robustness:

1. **Go (sender)**: In `convertBranchPath()` (`handler.go:320-338`), when `ConstraintJSON` is empty, emit an `Unknown` constraint with a descriptive hint instead of `nil`. Remove `omitempty` from the `Constraint` field tag.

2. **Rust (receiver)**: Add `#[serde(default)]` to the `constraint` field in `BranchDecision`, with a `Default` impl on `SymConstraint` that returns `Unknown { hint: String::new() }`. This makes the protocol resilient to any frontend that omits the field.

Both changes together ensure the protocol is robust in both directions.

title: Epic: Assemble genetic search pipeline in orchestrator
description:
Three sophisticated modules exist but are completely disconnected from the exploration loop:

1. genetic_fitness.rs — 4-axis fitness scoring (coverage, proximity, unknown_bonus, novelty), tested, never called outside tests
2. strategy.rs — InputStrategy trait + MetaStrategy with adaptive selection, feedback, hit-rate scoring, tested, never imported by orchestrator
3. input_gen.rs — type-aware mutate_inputs() and crossover_inputs(), tested, never called by orchestrator (which uses a naive hardcoded fuzz_inputs instead)

The orchestrator uses a BinaryHeap ordered solely by InputSource enum and a 30-line hardcoded fuzzer. Assembling these into a genetic search pipeline means: (a) fitness-scored worklist ordering so deeper/higher-coverage executions get priority, (b) adaptive strategy selection so budget flows to whichever generation method works best, (c) type-aware mutation and crossover between high-fitness parents. This is the difference between directed exploration and random walking.

Sub-issues: str-yvp4 (depth axis), str-hoyl (fitness→worklist), str-lebv (MetaStrategy integration), plus naive fuzzer replacement.

title: Solver declares string params as Int — pass ParamInfo to Z3 variable creation
description:
Root cause of str-omrx. infer_sort() in solver.rs:162 defaults SymExpr::Param to Sort::Int. When a string param appears inside .length (which returns Int), the param gets declared as Int in Z3. Z3 then solves for an integer, overlay_solved_values writes it back as json!(42), and the frontend crashes with 'email.indexOf is not a function'.

Fix: thread &[ParamInfo] into solve_for_new_path → to_z3_expr. When creating a Z3 variable for SymExpr::Param, look up the param name in ParamInfo and use its TypeInfo to select the correct Sort (Str→Sort::Str, Int→Sort::Int, Float→Sort::Real, Bool→Sort::Bool). Fall back to infer_sort only for sub-expressions like object paths that aren't top-level params.

Key files: solver.rs (infer_sort, to_z3_expr, solve_for_new_path), orchestrator.rs (calls solve_for_new_path).

Reproduction test already written: e2e_concolic.rs::concolic_validateemail_boundary_seeds_only

title: Add ExploreConfig.candidate_inputs field and wire into random explorer input loop
description:
This is the implementation task for str-emw6 (random explorer ignores --inputs).

Changes needed:
1. shatter-core/src/explorer.rs: Add 'candidate_inputs: Vec<Vec<serde_json::Value>>' field to ExploreConfig (line ~48, alongside pool_seeds)
2. shatter-core/src/explorer.rs: Insert candidate_inputs into the input generation priority chain (line ~343) between pool_seeds and custom generators. They should have priority above pool seeds but below literal seeds.
3. shatter-cli/src/main.rs: In the random explorer branch (~line 1132), convert resolved.candidate_inputs to Vec<Vec<Value>> (same pattern as concolic path at lines 1094-1097) and set explore_config.candidate_inputs.
4. Add a test in explorer.rs that verifies candidate inputs are executed before random generation kicks in.

Acceptance: running 'shatter explore 15-email-validator.ts --inputs seeds.json' where seeds.json contains {"args": ["test@example.com"]} must execute that input and discover the 'valid: true' path.

title: Parity enforcement: buildSymExprWithFlow must handle all node types that buildSymExpr handles
description:
instrumentor.ts has two functions that build symbolic expressions: buildSymExprWithFlow() (lines 278-352, used during data flow analysis) and buildSymExpr() (lines 860-951, used during branch condition analysis). They handle different sets of AST node types, causing silent data loss.

buildSymExpr() handles: Identifier, PropertyAccessExpression, CallExpression, PrefixUnaryExpression, BinaryExpression, ParenthesizedExpression, StringLiteral, NumericLiteral, and more.

buildSymExprWithFlow() handles: Identifier, PropertyAccessExpression, PrefixUnaryExpression, BinaryExpression, StringLiteral, NumericLiteral, and more — but NOT CallExpression.

This gap means any variable assigned from a method call becomes unknown in the flow map, and all subsequent references to it in branch conditions also become unknown. This is the root cause of str-sg5d.

Fix approach: Either refactor into a single shared function with a flag for context, or add a test that programmatically verifies both functions handle the same set of node types. The test approach is more robust since the two functions have legitimately different contexts (flow analysis vs. branch rewriting).

Add a unit test in instrumentor.test.ts that instruments a function with 'const x = s.indexOf("@"); if (x === -1) { ... }' and asserts the branch condition is NOT unknown.

title: E2E concolic test for string-heavy function (email validator)
description:
Add an integration test in shatter-core/tests/e2e_concolic.rs that runs the full concolic pipeline on examples/standalone/ts/15-email-validator.ts. This is the primary regression gate for the string ops → Z3 solver path.

The test should:
1. Spawn the real TS frontend
2. Analyze validateEmail to get 19 branches and string-method conditions
3. Instrument the function for symbolic tracking
4. Run concolic exploration with seed inputs including structurally valid emails (e.g. 'test@example.com', '@domain.com', 'user@', '.dot@x.com', 'user+tag@x.com')
5. Assert minimum path coverage — initially modest (e.g. ≥6 distinct paths) given known limitations (str-sg5d, str-omrx), with TODOs to raise the bar as those are fixed

Why this test matters:
- str-sg5d (data flow drops CallExpression) means most branch conditions are unknown → solver can't help
- str-omrx (cold-start problem) means concolic gets stuck without good seeds
- str-emw6 (candidate inputs ignored) means hand-crafted seeds don't reach the random explorer
- This single test would have caught ALL THREE bugs before they accumulated

The test should be tagged with #[ignore] initially if it requires str-sg5d to be fixed first, with a clear comment explaining why and which issue unblocks it. Pattern after existing concolic_classifynumber_discovers_all_branches test.

When str-sg5d and str-omrx are fixed, the threshold should be raised to ≥12 distinct paths (the 12 branches reachable without regex/for-loop analysis).

title: Data flow analysis drops CallExpression assignments to unknown
description: buildSymExprWithFlow() in instrumentor.ts:278-352 has no case for ts.isCallExpression(), so any local variable assigned from a method call (e.g. 'const local = email.slice(0, atIdx)') falls through to {kind: 'unknown'} at line 351. Since visitStatementsForDataFlow() filters out unknowns (line 236), these variables are never stored in the dataFlowMap. All subsequent branch conditions referencing these locals (local.length, domain.startsWith, labels.length, etc.) also become unknown, making them invisible to the Z3 solver. This is the primary reason the email validator gets only 4/20 branches — 13 of 19 branch conditions contain unknown nodes. Fix: add CallExpression handling to buildSymExprWithFlow() that mirrors buildSymExpr()'s call handling (lines 936-948), producing symbolic call nodes for method invocations on tracked expressions.

title: Random explorer ignores --inputs candidate inputs
description: The --inputs CLI flag loads candidate inputs (logged as 'N candidate input(s) from config') but the random explorer path in main.rs:1132 never passes them to explore_function(). Only the concolic orchestrator wires candidate_inputs into its worklist. The ExploreConfig struct in explorer.rs has no field for candidate inputs, and the input generation loop (explorer.rs:343-386) only uses literal seeds, pool seeds, custom generators, and random — never user-provided candidates. Fix: add candidate_inputs field to ExploreConfig, convert and pass them in main.rs, insert them into the input priority chain between pool seeds and generators.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 0f656e7 — user_seeds field added to ExploreConfig, wired in main.rs

title: Wire string method calls to Z3 string theory in solver
description:
The z3 Rust crate (v0.19) fully supports the Seq sort with contains, prefix, suffix, substr, length, at, concat, and indexof operations. Frontends already capture string method calls as SymExpr::Call { name, receiver, args }. The solver rejects all Call expressions at solver.rs line 248 with SolverError::Unsupported.

Implement ~10 match arms in to_z3_expr() mapping Call names to Z3 string operations:
- contains/includes → z3 String::contains
- indexOf/Index → z3 indexof (with -1 sentinel for not-found)
- startsWith/HasPrefix → z3 String::prefix (note: arg order flip)
- endsWith/HasSuffix → z3 String::suffix
- length/len → z3 String::length
- charAt/at → z3 String::at
- slice/substring/substr → z3 String::substr
- concat → z3 String::concat

Each frontend uses different method names for the same operation. Initially hard-code mappings for TS and Go (the two working frontends). This unblocks Z3-guided input generation for string-heavy functions like validateEmail.

This is the prerequisite for build-time table generation (separate issue) but delivers immediate value.

title: Line coverage denominator always 1 in explore output
description: When exploring, line coverage always reports X/1 lines (100%). E.g. 'Line coverage: 7/1 lines (100%)'. The total_lines is computed from end_line - start_line + 1, so if both are 0 or equal, total_lines=1. Need to investigate whether frontends are returning incorrect start_line/end_line, or whether the value isn't propagated correctly through the orchestrator→pipeline path.

title: Rust frontend: implement execute command
description: The Rust frontend's handle_execute() in handler.rs:305 returns 'not yet implemented'. This is the blocker for Rust exploration — without execute, the engine can't run instrumented functions with generated inputs. Needs: compile instrumented source, load and call the target function with provided inputs, capture return value/panic/side effects, report branch coverage via instrumentation hooks. This is the most complex piece — likely requires a subprocess or dylib-loading approach since Rust is compiled.
close_reason: Implemented: executor.rs subprocess pipeline, handle_execute wired up, 155 tests pass

title: Graceful error recovery when frontend crashes on unresolvable imports
description: During scan, files like 11-opaque-types.ts and 12-external-deps.ts crash the TS frontend process (broken pipe / closed stdout) when they try to require external modules like 'pg', 'net', etc. that aren't installed. Instead of the frontend process dying and leaving a broken pipe, it should catch module resolution errors during loadModule/executeInstrumented and return a structured protocol error (e.g. ErrorCode::InternalError with a clear message about the missing module). This would let the scan orchestrator skip that function gracefully and continue with the remaining functions, rather than losing the entire frontend subprocess. Currently causes ~68 errors in the walkthrough, all from scan steps exercising these files.
close_reason: Implemented async execution support, .catch() safety net, and dead frontend respawn

Uses sha2 crate (already a dep). Property tests for key reordering, whitespace, number normalization.

title: Generator Plugin System: WASM + Custom Build (Epic)

title: Z3 solver timeout
description: Both solve_for_new_path and solve_constraints in solver.rs create Config::new() with no params. Z3 crate 0.19 provides Config::set_timeout_msec(ms) — context-level timeout. When Z3 hits deadline, solver.check() returns SatResult::Unknown with reason 'timeout'; check_and_extract already handles this as SolverError::Unknown. Add solver_timeout_ms: Option<u64> param to both solve functions. Apply cfg.set_timeout_msec(ms) when Some. Add solver_timeout_ms to ExploreConfig (explorer.rs) and thread through orchestrator.rs:396. Add --solver-timeout CLI flag (optional, seconds) to Explore/Scan/Run. Add test: 1ms timeout on satisfiable constraint → either Sat or Unknown (no panic).

title: Implement --timeout-total for scan command
description: --timeout-total is parsed in CLI (default 300s) but stored as _timeout_total (unused). Add timeout_total: Option<Duration> to ScanConfig. In parallel_scan, record scan_start = Instant::now() before layer loop. At top of each layer iteration, check elapsed >= total; if exceeded, push remaining functions as skipped with reason 'total scan timeout exceeded' and break. Layer-boundary check preserves partial results. Rename _timeout_total to timeout_total in run_scan. Pass Some(Duration::from_secs(timeout_total)) to ScanConfig (0 = None). Add test: parallel_scan with timeout_total=Duration::ZERO → all functions skipped.

title: TS frontend: read SHATTER_EXEC_TIMEOUT and apply vm.runInContext timeout
description: The TS frontend ignores SHATTER_EXEC_TIMEOUT entirely. Add getExecTimeoutMs() helper that reads the env var (default 15s). Apply { timeout: N } to both vm.runInContext calls in executor.ts (lines 86, 397). Add unit tests: default value, env var parsing, invalid value handling, module-level infinite loop aborted by timeout. Export getExecTimeoutMs for testing.

title: Frontend feature parity matrix documentation

Create a PARITY.md (or a section in PLAN.md) that lists every protocol command and feature with implementation status per frontend (TypeScript, Go, Rust). Columns: Feature | TS | Go | Rust | Notes. Cover at minimum: handshake, analyze, instrument, execute, setup, teardown, generate, shutdown, side-effect capture (console, file, network, env, global mutation, thrown error, global state change), symbolic constraint reporting, literal extraction, perf metrics, mock support, truncation policy. Update this matrix whenever a frontend adds or changes a feature. This serves as the acceptance criteria for frontend completeness.

description: The test panics with 'failed to set permissions on go frontend binary: No such file or directory'. The test relies on the embedded Go frontend binary existing at extraction time but the binary may not be present in all test environments. Needs to either mock the extraction or ensure the binary is available.

title: Make-friendly per-file specs with fingerprint-based incremental exploration
description:
## Problem

Shatter's CLI currently bundles discovery, analysis, and exploration into monolithic commands (scan, run) with outputs going to hidden .shatter/cache/ or shatter-report/. This makes it unusable with Make, which needs predictable input→output file relationships with timestamp-based staleness.

The lasting output of Shatter is behavioral specifications — not human reports. Specs are the precise, complete, machine-diffable records of observed behavior. They should be first-class project artifacts, not hidden cache.

## Design

### Two first-class artifacts, per-source-file granularity

1. **Spec files** (FunctionSpec[]) — the lasting, diffable behavioral specification. Versioned in git. Used by spec-diff for regression detection.
2. **Behavior map files** (BehaviorMap[]) — input→output mappings for compositional mocking. Stored separately, may or may not be versioned.

Both are per-source-file: specs/src/math.ts.json contains specs for all functions in math.ts.

### Internal function fingerprinting for performance

When a source file changes, Shatter doesn't blindly re-explore all functions:
1. Run cheap analyze phase → extract function signatures/ASTs
2. Compute per-function fingerprint (hash of source text, parameter types, branch structure)
3. Compare against fingerprints stored in existing output
4. Only re-explore functions with changed fingerprints
5. Merge results: preserve unchanged specs, update changed ones

Fingerprints should compose across the call graph: if B's behavior changes, A (which calls B) needs re-exploration even if A's source is unchanged.

### CLI changes needed

1. **explore --output <path>** — write spec to explicit path (Make target)
2. **explore accepts file-level targets** — shatter explore src/math.ts explores all functions in the file
3. **shatter stale** — lightweight staleness check (exit code 0/1) using fingerprints, for non-Make incremental workflows
4. **explore --clean** — ignore existing specs/fingerprints and force full re-exploration from scratch. Escape hatch for when fingerprints are stale, cache is corrupted, or exploration heuristics have changed.
5. **explore --dry-run** — run analyze + fingerprint comparison, print what would be explored (which functions are stale, which would be skipped), but don't actually run the concolic engine. Useful for understanding what Make would trigger, debugging fingerprint behavior, and CI cost estimation.

### Makefile usage

```makefile
SPECS_DIR := specs

$(SPECS_DIR)/src/math.ts.json: src/math.ts
    shatter explore src/math.ts -o $@

$(SPECS_DIR)/src/utils.ts.json: src/utils.ts
    shatter explore src/utils.ts -o $@
```

### What stays the same

- scan and run remain as batteries-included commands for users not using Make
- .shatter/cache/ remains for internal caching (behavior maps for compositional mocking during exploration)

title: Date/DateTime/Duration support end-to-end
description:
Full support for temporal types: generators, analyzer recognition, reconstruction, and integration test.

## What
1. Core generators (input_gen.rs): generate_date and generate_duration with boundary-biased values
   - Date boundaries: epoch 0, negative epoch, Y2K38 (2038-01-19), 2024-01-01, month/year boundaries, far future (9999-12-31), far past (0001-01-01)
   - Duration boundaries: 0, negative, 1ms, 1s, 1min, 1hr, 1day, MAX_SAFE_INTEGER
   - Wire format: {"__complex_type": "date", "value": <epoch_ms>}, {"__complex_type": "duration", "ms": <millis>}

2. TS analyzer (shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts): Recognize Date type in convertType/convertObjectType by checking type symbol name
3. TS reconstruction (new shatter-ts/src/reconstruct.ts): reconstructValue() infrastructure + Date case (new Date(epoch_ms))
4. Go analyzer (shatter-go/protocol/analyzer.go): Recognize time.Time and time.Duration
5. Go reconstruction (new shatter-go/reconstruct/reconstruct.go): time.UnixMilli(ms) and time.Duration(ms)*time.Millisecond

6. Integration test: isWeekend(date: Date): boolean — function with two paths (weekday/weekend), verify solver generates dates hitting both paths

## Solver
ConstValue::Complex { kind: Date, repr: Int(epoch_ms) } — solver unwraps to integer comparison. Already handled by the generic Complex unwrap from str-t8b.

## Priority
Highest real-world impact — Date appears in nearly every business application.

## Files
- shatter-core/src/input_gen.rs
- shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts
- shatter-ts/src/reconstruct.ts (new)
- shatter-ts/src/executor.ts (call reconstructValue before execution)
- shatter-go/protocol/analyzer.go
- shatter-go/reconstruct/reconstruct.go (new)
- examples/typescript/src/ (isWeekend test function)
close_reason: Date/DateTime/Duration generators added to input_gen.rs with boundary-biased values. TS analyzer recognizes Date, RegExp, URL, Error, Buffer, BigInt, Symbol via complexKindFromSymbol(). Go analyzer recognizes time.Time, time.Duration, url.URL, regexp.Regexp, net.IP, big.Int/Rat/Float via complexKindFromNamed(). reconstruct.ts and reconstruct.go created with full value reconstruction. Both frontends declare complex_type capabilities in handshake. 433 core + 142 TS + all Go tests pass. Clippy and go vet clean.

This fixes the fundamental issue in str-798 (every distinct return value treated as unique path) at the conceptual level and is prerequisite for meaningful specification generation.

title: Test case prioritization: order tests by marginal coverage per unit time
description:
Order tests by bug-catching ROI to maximize coverage reached per second of execution. Composes with TIA (str-zwgc) as a downstream stage: TIA selects affected tests, prioritization orders them, optional budget cutoff stops early.

## Algorithm

Greedy submodular maximization ("additional greedy"):
1. Score each test: (lines not yet covered by selected tests) / execution_time
2. Pick highest-scoring test, add to ordered list, mark its lines as covered
3. Recalculate scores for remaining tests (marginal coverage decreased)
4. Repeat until all tests ordered

O(n^2) with n=1,487 tests is about 2M comparisons, well under a second. Provably within (1 - 1/e) of optimal; in practice near-optimal at this scale.

## Data requirements

Extends the TIA coverage map (str-zwgc) with:
- Per-test execution time (every runner already reports this)
- Line-level coverage per test (not just file-level)
- Unique line count per test (lines no other test covers)
- Precomputed priority score: unique_lines / duration_s

Priority scores recomputed whenever the map refreshes (after any coverage-instrumented test run). Single pass over the map.

## Change recency weighting

Bias scores toward recently-changed code where bugs concentrate. Weight covered lines by days_since_last_change of their file (git log -1 --format=%ct -- file). Lines in recently-changed files get a multiplier. This makes the ordering adapt to what the developer is actively working on.

## Pipeline composition with TIA

All tests (1,487) -> TIA (str-zwgc): filter to change-affected -> Affected tests (~50-200) -> Prioritize: order by marginal coverage/time -> Ordered sequence -> Budget cutoff (optional): stop after N seconds -> First k tests that fit budget

The coverage map serves both stages. TIA uses file-to-test for selection, prioritization uses line-level coverage plus timing for ordering. One data structure, two optimizations.

## Flags

- shatter test --budget=10s: run prioritized tests until time budget exhausted
- shatter test --prioritize: run all affected tests but in priority order (fail-fast: most likely to catch bugs runs first)

## Prerequisites

- TIA coverage map infrastructure (str-zwgc)
- Baseline coverage measurement (never been run on this codebase)
- cargo-nextest for per-test filtering and timing

title: Test impact analysis: coverage-based selective test execution
description:
Implement test impact analysis (TIA) to accelerate local development by running only the tests affected by changed files. The system maintains a coverage map recording which tests touch which source files, then uses that map to select the minimal test set for a given set of changes.

## Design

**Coverage map** stored at `.shatter/test-markers/coverage-map.yaml`:
- Forward map: test → {file: blob_hash} (update direction)
- Reverse map: file → [tests] (query direction, derived from forward)
- Blob hashes via `git hash-object` for content-addressable comparison

**Query flow**: `git diff --name-only` → changed files → reverse lookup → union of affected tests → format runner-specific filter

**Update flow**: after test run with coverage instrumentation, parse coverage output → update forward map entries for tests that ran → rebuild reverse map. Atomic write via temp file + rename.

**Staleness tracking**: each file entry records the blob hash at recording time. Changed hash = stale = must re-run associated tests. Import-change detection: if a file's imports changed (not just function bodies), broadly invalidate its dependents.

**Cold start**: empty map treats all files as unmapped → falls back to crate/package-scoped testing. Map populates incrementally as tests run with coverage.

**Tiered test markers**: tree hash (from `git write-tree`) stored at `.shatter/test-markers/<tier>` after a tier's tests pass. Pre-commit hook compares current tree hash against the required tier's marker.

## Flags

- `shatter test` (or wrapper script) — run only affected tests based on coverage map
- `shatter test --all` — run all tests regardless, bypassing TIA selection (always available as escape hatch)
- `shatter test --record` — force coverage recording to refresh the map
- `shatter test --tier=<quick|standard|full|e2e>` — run the specified tier and write its marker on success

## Runner integration

- Rust: `cargo-nextest` with `-E 'test(=name)'` filter expressions (requires installing cargo-nextest). Falls back to `cargo test -- name1 name2` for module-level filtering.
- TypeScript: Vitest with explicit file paths or `--testNamePattern`
- Go: `go test ./package/ -run 'TestA|TestB'`

## Coverage collection

LLVM source-based coverage via `cargo-llvm-cov --json` for Rust (~10-20% overhead). Vitest `--coverage` for TS. `go test -coverprofile` for Go. For local dev loops where tests take 5-15s, always-on coverage is acceptable (~1-3s overhead). Alternatively, periodic recording with `--record` flag.

## Current test landscape (for reference)

- 1,487 Rust tests across ~60 modules + 3 integration test files
- 195 Go tests across protocol/instrument/generators packages
- 6 TS test files
- No cargo-nextest installed yet
- No cross-cutting test groups; filtering is by module/crate/package name
- Existing tiers: Quick (cargo test), Standard (+clippy), Full (+TS+Go), E2E (+e2e_concolic), Walkthrough (+demo script)

title: Add remaining high-value contracts to solver bridge and protocol boundary
description:
The contracts crate was added in str-xlo9 with 3 annotations. The contracts policy (shatter-core/CLAUDE.md) identifies 5 qualifying sites. Two are annotated; three remain:

1. solve_for_new_path() postcondition: solved value JSON types match ParamInfo::type_info. The invariant str-6ayh violated. Proptest only catches this if someone writes a type-checking property; the contract catches it on every call path including E2E.

2. to_z3_expr() precondition: all SymExpr::Param nodes reference valid indices in the param list. Invalid index causes Z3 to use the wrong variable, producing silently wrong solutions.

3. Protocol validation wrappers: create validate_execute_result() and validate_analyze_result() with postconditions — branch_path non-empty, param_values length matches arity, function signatures have valid param counts. These functions don't exist today (serde types are used directly); creating them is the deliverable.

NOT in scope: expanding beyond the 5 qualifying sites, orchestrator state, pure functions, or anything proptest covers.

title: Fuzz targets for shatter-core Rust deserialization boundaries
description:
Go has 10 fuzz targets finding real bugs. Rust shatter-core has zero fuzz targets — only proptest (which uses structured generation, not byte-level mutation). Add cargo-fuzz or proptest bytes-based fuzzing for:

1. Protocol JSON parsing: arbitrary bytes → serde_json::from_slice::<Request>() never panics.
2. SymExpr deserialization: arbitrary bytes → serde_json::from_slice::<SymExpr>() never panics.
3. TypeInfo deserialization: same for TypeInfo (recursive type, high surface area).
4. YAML spec parsing: arbitrary bytes → serde_yaml::from_slice never panics on spec types.
5. String ops YAML loading: malformed string-ops.yaml content → build.rs/solver gracefully errors.

The Go fuzz tests found a real timeout overflow bug. Rust deserialization has similar untested boundaries.

title: Go semantic property tests beyond roundtrips
description:
All Go PBT is roundtrip serialization. No behavioral properties on:

1. Analysis: arbitrary Go source snippets (valid function signatures) → analyzer extracts correct parameter count and types. Use rapid to generate simple Go function signatures.
2. Instrumentation: instrumented code preserves original function behavior — for simple functions, executing instrumented vs original with same inputs produces same output.
3. Mock generation: sanitizeMockName is idempotent (sanitize(sanitize(x)) == sanitize(x)) and always produces valid Go identifiers (starts with letter/underscore, contains only alphanumeric/underscore).
4. Executor: for any valid function + valid inputs, executor either returns a result or a structured error (never panics, never hangs past timeout).
5. Protocol handler: for any sequence of valid protocol messages, handler produces valid responses in the same order (no reordering, no drops).

title: Proptest coverage for input_gen mutation and shrink at vector level
description:
Current PBT on input_gen has only 4 trivial type-preservation checks (mutate int→number, str→string, bool→bool, vec length). No properties on:

1. mutate_inputs (vector-level): arbitrary (Vec<Value>, Vec<ParamInfo>) → output vec same length, each element matches its ParamInfo type, at least one element differs from input (mutation actually mutates).
2. crossover_inputs: arbitrary parent pairs → child has same length as parents, each element type-compatible with ParamInfo.
3. shrink_candidates: arbitrary (Value, TypeInfo) → all candidates are valid for the TypeInfo, none equal the original, candidates are strictly simpler (for numeric: abs(candidate) <= abs(original), for strings: len(candidate) <= len(original), for arrays: len(candidate) <= len(original)).
4. Generator-aware mutation: when generators provide value_sources, mutated values should still satisfy generator constraints.
5. Idempotent shrinking: shrinking an already-minimal value (0, empty string, false, null) produces empty candidates vec.

title: Proptest coverage for orchestrator state machine and worklist ordering
description:
The orchestrator has only 3 properties on overlay_solved_values. No PBT coverage on:

1. Worklist ordering: arbitrary WorklistEntry insertions → entries dequeue in priority order (UserProvided > Z3Solved > Fuzzed > Seed).
2. Path deduplication: executing the same branch path twice → covered_paths set prevents re-exploration (second execution doesn't add to worklist).
3. Constraint accumulation: after N executions with symbolic constraints, the constraint set grows monotonically (constraints are never lost).
4. Budget exhaustion: with budget=N, explore loop terminates after at most N executions.
5. Convergence: with a finite set of reachable paths, explore eventually stops finding new paths (no infinite loops).

These properties test the core exploration loop invariants that unit tests can't easily cover.

title: Proptest coverage for spec and export roundtrips
description:
Spec/export was listed in str-ho1d but not delivered. Zero PBT on:

1. format_spec_yaml roundtrip: arbitrary FunctionSpec → YAML serialize → deserialize → assert structural equality.
2. format_spec_json roundtrip: same for JSON output.
3. format_spec_markdown: arbitrary FunctionSpec → markdown output is non-empty, contains function name, contains all class labels.
4. Export test generation: arbitrary BehaviorMap → generated test cases reference valid function names and use valid input values.
5. Invariant conversion: arbitrary ClassifiedInvariant → SpecInvariant conversion preserves label text, maps confidence correctly (≥0.95→High, ≥0.75→Medium, else Low), maps kind to valid InvariantCategory.

title: Document formal methods policy in CLAUDE.md
description: After implementing proptest and contracts, add a Formal Methods section to the root CLAUDE.md documenting: when to use proptest/fast-check/rapid (liberal — any non-trivial invariant), when to use contracts (high bar — only where types can't express the invariant), when to consider Kani (deferred — only highest-stakes properties), the relationship between these tools, per-component guidance (proptest in shatter-core, fast-check in shatter-ts, rapid in shatter-go), and anti-patterns (contracts that restate types, proptest for trivial functions). Could be folded into str-ho1d and str-xlo9 rather than a separate issue.

title: Fallback boundary search for unknown/opaque constraints
description: When Z3 returns unknown or timeout for a constraint, and Shatter has inputs on both sides of a branch, use coverage-guided binary-search-style shrinking to find the boundary concretely. Currently falls back to naive fuzz_inputs() which is blind. Requires one input on each side (true-path witness, false-path witness). Interpolate/mutate between them, re-execute, track which side each result takes. More effective than random fuzzing because it has directional signal. Uses same shrink_candidates infrastructure. Relates to str-e0a4 (type-aware mutation), str-e0uy (hybrid concolic + fuzzing).

title: Minimal witness shrinking
description: After discovering an input that triggers a behavior, shrink it to the simplest input that still triggers the same behavior. Analogous to QuickCheck's shrinking but executed via frontend subprocess protocol. Try removing characters, reducing numbers toward zero, shortening arrays, removing optional object fields. Re-execute via frontend to confirm behavior is preserved. Stop when no simpler input preserves the behavior. Produces cleaner test cases, better seeds for interesting pool, more readable behavioral specs. Shares shrink_candidates(value, type_info) -> Vec<Value> function with boundary refinement. Depends on str-e0a4 (type-aware mutation infrastructure).

title: Boundary refinement phase with separate budget
description: After the discovery phase (find distinct behaviors), run a separate refinement phase that binary-searches between known inputs on opposite sides of each branch to find precise transition points. Separate budget from discovery (e.g. --refine-budget 20 per boundary, distinct from --max-executions 100). Takes discovered behaviors + witness inputs as input. For each pair of adjacent behaviors, searches for the minimal input that transitions between them. Produces precise boundary descriptions in the behavioral spec. Uses type-aware shrink candidates (same infrastructure as type-aware mutation from str-e0a4). Depends on str-e0a4 (type-aware mutation provides shrink_candidates).

title: Shrink candidates function (shared infrastructure)
description: Implement shrink_candidates(value: &Value, type_info: &TypeInfo) -> Vec<Value> — the inverse of mutate_inputs. Given a value, produce progressively simpler variants: Numbers: halve toward zero, try 0, 1, -1. Strings: remove last/first char, try empty, single char. Booleans: try false. Arrays: remove last/first element, try empty. Objects: remove optional fields one at a time. Nullable: try null. Shared by both minimal witness shrinking and boundary refinement. Related to but distinct from str-e0a4's mutation — mutation goes toward novel, shrinking goes toward simpler.

Parent epic for all formal methods work in Shatter.

title: Fallback boundary search for unknown/opaque constraints
description:
When Z3 returns unknown or timeout for a constraint, and Shatter has inputs on both sides of a branch, use coverage-guided binary-search-style shrinking to find the boundary concretely. Currently falls back to naive fuzz_inputs() which is blind.

Prerequisites:
- Need one input on each side (true-path witness, false-path witness)
- Need shrink_candidates infrastructure

Algorithm:
1. Start with input A (true branch) and input B (false branch)
2. Interpolate/mutate A toward B using type-aware candidates
3. Re-execute each candidate
4. Track which side of the branch each result takes
5. Converge on transition point

More effective than random fuzzing because it has directional signal. This is essentially delta debugging applied to input space.

No code reuse from proptest — shared algorithmic pattern but entirely different execution model (subprocess protocol vs in-process function calls).

Related: str-e0a4 (type-aware mutation), str-e0uy (hybrid concolic + fuzzing)
notes: Epic: str-08r7 (Formal methods and property-based verification)

title: Boundary refinement phase with separate execution budget
description:
After the discovery phase (find distinct behaviors), run a separate refinement phase that binary-searches between known inputs on opposite sides of each branch to find precise transition points.

Key design:
- SEPARATE budget from discovery (e.g. --refine-budget N per boundary, distinct from --max-executions)
- Discovery benefits from breadth (spread inputs across the domain)
- Refinement benefits from focus (narrow in on a known boundary)
- Mixing them in the same loop with the same budget means one steals from the other

Algorithm per boundary:
1. Take two inputs: one triggering behavior A, one triggering behavior B
2. Interpolate/mutate between them using shrink_candidates
3. Re-execute, track which behavior each result triggers
4. Converge on the precise transition point

Output:
- Precise boundary descriptions in behavioral spec
- Minimal inputs at each transition point
- Higher-quality seeds for the interesting pool

Shatter should prioritize finding precise boundaries — this is a distinct phase, not mixed into discovery.
notes: Epic: str-08r7 (Formal methods and property-based verification)

title: Minimal witness shrinking: simplify discovered inputs
description:
After discovering an input that triggers a behavior, shrink it to the simplest input that still triggers the same behavior.

Algorithm:
1. Take an input that triggers behavior B
2. For each parameter, try shrink candidates (simpler values)
3. Re-execute via frontend; if still triggers B, keep the simpler version
4. Stop when no further simplification preserves B

Benefits:
- Cleaner, more readable test cases in export output
- Better seeds for the interesting pool (simpler seeds mutate more productively)
- More readable behavioral specs
- Minimal witnesses are the most informative examples of each behavior

Uses shrink_candidates function. No code reuse from proptest — shared algorithmic pattern (directed search via execution feedback) but execution goes through Shatter's frontend subprocess protocol, not in-process.
notes: Epic: str-08r7 (Formal methods and property-based verification)

title: Shrink candidates function: type-aware input simplification
description:
Implement shrink_candidates(value: &Value, type_info: &TypeInfo) -> Vec<Value> — given a value and its type, produce progressively simpler variants. This is the inverse of mutation (toward simpler, not toward novel).

Shrink strategies by type:
- Numbers: halve toward zero, try 0, try 1, try -1
- Strings: remove last char, remove first char, try empty, try single char
- Booleans: try false
- Arrays: remove last element, remove first element, try empty
- Objects: remove optional fields one at a time
- Nullable: try null

Shared infrastructure for minimal witness shrinking and boundary refinement. Related to but distinct from str-e0a4's type-aware mutation.
notes: Epic: str-08r7 (Formal methods and property-based verification)

title: Add contracts crate to shatter-core solver bridge and trust boundaries
description:
Add the contracts crate (design-by-contract) to shatter-core with a HIGH bar for usage — only where Rust's type system genuinely cannot express the invariant.

## Where contracts are warranted

### Solver bridge (primary target)
Crosses Rust/Z3 FFI boundary where Rust types can't help — Z3 sorts are runtime values.

- solve_for_new_path(): Pre: all Param nodes reference valid param indices. Post: solved values have types matching ParamInfo.
- infer_sort(): Post: returned Sort matches the TypeInfo of the referenced parameter.
- overlay_solved_values(): Pre: solved_values keys are valid param indices. Post: output length equals input length. Post: each output value's JSON type matches ParamInfo's TypeInfo.
- to_z3_expr(): Pre: SymExpr tree is well-formed (no dangling Param references).

### Protocol deserialization boundary
JSON from frontend subprocesses — types can't guarantee structural validity:

- parse_execute_result(): Post: branch_ids are non-empty, param_values length matches arity.
- parse_analyze_result(): Post: function signatures have valid parameter counts.

### Orchestrator state transitions

- add_to_worklist(): Post: worklist is non-empty after add.
- mark_path_covered(): Post: covered_paths contains the new hash.

### Where contracts are NOT warranted

- Functions where the type signature already encodes the contract
- Simple transformations where proptest is more valuable
- Internal helpers with a single call site
- Any function where the pre/postcondition restates the type signature

## Relationship with Kani

Complementary: contracts document intent inline and catch violations at runtime (debug builds). Kani proves contracts hold for ALL inputs statically. Write the contract first, optionally add Kani harness later for the most critical ones.

## Documentation

Add section to shatter-core/CLAUDE.md explaining the contracts usage policy with the high bar. Include examples of good contracts (solver bridge) vs bad contracts (restating types).
notes: Epic: str-08r7 (Formal methods and property-based verification)

title: Add proptest to shatter-core, fast-check to shatter-ts, rapid to shatter-go
description:
Add property-based testing across all Shatter components. Liberal usage policy — use proptest/fast-check/rapid for any non-trivial function where interesting invariants exist.

## Rust (proptest in shatter-core)

Add proptest as a dev-dependency. Priority targets:

1. Protocol roundtrips: arbitrary Request/Response → serialize → deserialize → assert equality.
2. SymExpr sort inference: arbitrary SymExpr trees with known param types → assert infer_sort() returns correct Z3 sort. Prevents str-6ayh class of bug.
3. Input mutation type preservation: arbitrary (TypeInfo, Value) pairs → mutate → assert output matches type contract.
4. Path hash determinism: arbitrary BranchOutcome sequences → assert path_hash is deterministic.
5. Overlay solved values: arbitrary param lists and solved value maps → assert overlay preserves vector length and type compatibility.
6. Spec/export roundtrips.

### When to use proptest (guidance for contributors)

Use proptest when a function has:
- Roundtrip property (serialize/deserialize, encode/decode, parse/format)
- Invariant that should hold for ALL inputs (length preservation, type preservation, monotonicity)
- Multiple code paths that should produce equivalent results (parallel path parity)
- Boundary behavior hard to enumerate by hand
- Any function touching the solver bridge

Don't bother for: simple getters/setters, thin wrappers, tests easier as specific examples.

## TypeScript (fast-check in shatter-ts)

Add fast-check as a devDependency. Priority targets:
1. Protocol message roundtrips.
2. SymExpr builder consistency (buildSymExpr and buildSymExprWithFlow parity).
3. Instrumentor output validity.

fast-check properties are executable TypeScript using fc.property() with vitest. No DSL.

## Go (rapid in shatter-go)

Add rapid as a test dependency. Priority targets:
1. Protocol message roundtrips.
2. Executor timeout parsing (arbitrary env var strings → valid duration or default).
3. Analysis result consistency.

Also use native Go fuzzing (testing.F) for parsing/deserialization code.

## Documentation

Add sections to each component's CLAUDE.md explaining the PBT usage policy with examples of good properties and anti-patterns.
notes: Epic: str-08r7 (Formal methods and property-based verification)

title: Float probe: detect integer-treating number parameters via behavioral testing
description:
## Feature

When a TypeScript function accepts `number`, shatter can't distinguish integer-intended from float-intended parameters. This leads to noisy float inputs like `intToRoman(410.50190091454465)` where `intToRoman(410)` would be more useful, and misses the interesting edge case that `intToRoman(0.5)` silently returns `""` instead of throwing.

## Design

### Detection
Early in exploration, run ~5 float probes alongside their `Math.floor()` equivalents. If `f(x) == f(floor(x))` for ≥N% of probes (configurable threshold, default 80%), classify the parameter as 'integer-treating.'

### Input generation
For integer-treating parameters:
- Generate ~M% integers, ~(100-M)% floats (configurable ratio, default 80/20)
- The float budget targets boundary values: `0.5`, `-0.1`, `N.999` — values where the fractional part is most likely to change behavior

### Reporting
- Show `floor()` equivalent as the canonical example: `intToRoman(410)` not `intToRoman(410.50)`
- If a float input produces a DIFFERENT result from its floor, flag it prominently as a domain validation finding
- Example: `(0.5) -> "" — non-integer input bypasses validation (floor equivalent (0) THROWS Error: out of range)`

### Why not suppress floats entirely
Float inputs that diverge from their floor equivalent reveal real bugs (missing input validation). The 20% float budget is where the tool earns its keep — `intToRoman(0.5) -> ""` is exactly the kind of surprise shatter exists to surface.

## Configuration

Configurable via `--set` (see str-TBD for the `--set` system) or YAML config:

- `explore.float_probe.threshold`: confidence threshold for integer classification (default: 0.8)
- `explore.float_probe.integer_ratio`: fraction of integer inputs when integer-treating detected (default: 0.8)
- `explore.float_probe.probes`: number of float/floor probe pairs to run (default: 5)

These are too obscure for top-level CLI flags; they belong in the `--set` / YAML config system.
notes: Config system dependency: str-nh7l (--set flag). Complementary static heuristic: str-nfg2.

title: Scan report mixes expected skips (opaque types, cache hits) with actual errors
description:
## Problem

The scan report lumps all skipped functions into a single 'Skipped functions:' section with no visual distinction between:

1. **Expected skips** — opaque parameter types (`net.Socket`, `stream.Readable`), fingerprint cache hits, checkpoint resumes
2. **Actual errors** — exploration errors, deserialization failures, frontend crashes, timeouts

In a real codebase, many functions will have opaque types (DB connections, sockets, file handles). These are normal and expected — they're not actionable failures. But they currently look identical to real errors in the report.

## Current output

```
Skipped functions:
  handleRequest: param "socket" has opaque type net.Socket
  processStream: param "input" has opaque type stream.Readable
  authenticate: error: exploration error: unexpected response from frontend
```

## Expected behavior

The report should visually separate these categories. For example:

```
Skipped (opaque types, 2):
  handleRequest: param "socket" has opaque type net.Socket
  processStream: param "input" has opaque type stream.Readable

Errors (1):
  authenticate: exploration error: unexpected response from frontend
```

Or at minimum, prefix reasons with a category tag so automated tools (and AI agents reviewing output) can distinguish them.

## Impact

AI agents running the walkthrough or reviewing scan output can't distinguish normal opaque-type skips from real bugs. This was a contributing factor in pipeline bugs going undetected — the agent sees 'Skipped functions' and either ignores all of them (missing real errors) or flags all of them (drowning signal in noise).

title: Symbolic constraint count is inflated — counts per-execution observations, not unique constraints
description:
## Bug

The 'Symbolic expr' line in coverage metrics reports inflated constraint counts that vary between runs. For `categorizeUser` (5 branches), it shows values like 189, 194, 196, or 203 — and the number changes every run.

## Root Cause

`pipeline.rs:45-54` collects constraints from **every execution's branch path** without deduplication:

```rust
let all_constraints: Vec<SymConstraint> = observe
    .raw_results
    .iter()
    .flat_map(|(_, result)| {
        result.branch_path.iter().map(|d| d.constraint.clone())
    })
    .collect();
```

With 100 iterations, each execution contributes its branch path's constraints. An execution hitting 2 branches adds 2 entries; one hitting 4 branches adds 4. This produces ~200 total constraint observations for a function with only 5 unique branch conditions.

## Why it varies

Different random inputs take different paths. Some runs may have more executions hitting longer paths (3-4 branches) vs shorter ones (1 branch for the throw path), so the total observation count is nondeterministic.

## Expected behavior

The metric should report the number of **unique branch constraints** that are SymExpr vs Unknown — i.e., how many of the function's branch points have solvable symbolic expressions. For `categorizeUser` this should be ~5, not ~200.

## Fix

Deduplicate constraints by branch_id before counting. Each branch_id should contribute exactly one constraint classification (Expr or Unknown) regardless of how many times it was observed across executions.

title: Line coverage denominator counts non-executable lines, systematically undercounting coverage
description:
## Bug

`total_lines` is computed as `end_line - start_line + 1` (explorer.rs:711), which counts ALL lines in the function span — including blank lines, closing braces, the function signature, and empty lines. But `lines_covered` only counts lines where `__shatter_record(line)` was inserted by the instrumentor, which only fires for executable statements.

This means line coverage can **never reach 100%** for any function. The more structural lines a function has (braces, blanks), the worse the reported coverage — even when every executable statement is hit.

## Example

`intToRoman` (13-roman-numerals.ts:52-68) reports 9/17 (53%) despite all executable statements being covered. The 8 'uncovered' lines are: function signature (52), closing braces (55, 64, 65, 68), and blank lines (56, 59, 66).

## Root Cause

Mismatched domains: the numerator counts instrumented statement lines, the denominator counts raw source lines.

## Fix

Have the frontend report the number of **instrumentable lines** (statements that received a `__shatter_record()` call) in the analysis response or execute response, and use that as the denominator instead of `end_line - start_line + 1`. Alternatively, have the Rust side compute executable line count from the analysis data (branch conditions + statement count).

title: Triage unit and integration tests
description: Unit tests in triage.rs: evaluate_constraint with numeric comparisons, string ops, field paths, Unknown->None, unsupported Call->None. predict_branch mapping to Taken/NotTaken/Indeterminate. TriageState::update deduplication and MAX_BRANCH_TRACES cap. triage_candidate returning correct verdicts for redundant/novel/unknown inputs. is_worthwhile false at low skip rate. record_sample disabling at high misprediction rate. Integration: existing orchestrator tests must pass unchanged. New orchestrator test verifying triage_skipped > 0 with redundant worklist entries. E2E: cargo test --test e2e_concolic must discover same paths with triage active.

title: Wire triage into orchestrator explore() loop
description: Integrate triage into orchestrator.rs explore() function: (1) Create TriageState::new() after worklist init. (2) Call triage_state.update() after each new-path execution (after executions.push). (3) Before frontend.send(Command::Execute), check triage verdict — skip UserProvided inputs, continue on Skip (with sampling), proceed on Execute/Indeterminate. (4) On sampled Skip executions, call record_sample() to track mispredictions. (5) Add triage_skipped and triage_mispredictions fields to ExploreResult. (6) Register pub mod triage in lib.rs. Triage skips must NOT increment plateau_counter or total_executions.
close_reason: Triage wired into orchestrator explore() loop. All 7 acceptance criteria met. E2E passing.

title: Adaptive triage self-disabling (skip-rate and misprediction)
description: Two mechanisms to auto-disable triage when it is not useful: (1) Skip-rate check: after 20 verdicts, if fewer than 10% result in Skip, disable triage — it is not producing value. Covers functions with mostly Unknown constraints or highly productive exploration. (2) Misprediction check: every 20th Skip verdict, execute anyway (sampling) and compare predicted path hash to actual. If misprediction rate exceeds 25% of samples, the constraint model is unreliable — disable triage. record_sample() tracks this. Constants: TRIAGE_SAMPLE_INTERVAL=20, MIN_VERDICTS_FOR_EVAL=20, MIN_SKIP_RATE=0.10, MAX_MISPREDICTION_RATE=0.25.

title: TriageState and triage_candidate verdict logic
description: TriageState accumulates branch traces (ordered branch_id + constraint sequences from observed executions, deduped by branch-ID sequence, capped at 64) and per-branch observed directions. triage_candidate() walks each trace, evaluates constraints via evaluate_constraint, and returns a TriageVerdict: Skip (predicted path hash in covered_paths), Execute (predicted novel branch direction with novel_count and first_novel_depth), or Indeterminate (too many unknowns or no matching trace). Includes predict_branch() helper that maps evaluate_constraint results to BranchPrediction enum (Taken/NotTaken/Indeterminate). Also includes TriageState::update() called after each new-path execution to record traces and observed directions.

title: Concrete SymExpr evaluator (evaluate_constraint)
description: Pure interpreter that walks a SymExpr tree and evaluates it against concrete JSON parameter values. Returns Option<Value> — None for Unknown nodes or unsupported operations. Supports: Param lookup with field-path traversal, BinOp comparisons (Eq/Ne/Lt/Le/Gt/Ge), arithmetic (Add/Sub/Mul/Div/Mod), logical (And/Or with short-circuit), UnOp (Not/Neg), and Call for the 8 canonical string ops (contains/includes, startsWith/prefix, endsWith/suffix, indexOf/index_of, length/len). Lives in new shatter-core/src/triage.rs. No existing concrete evaluator exists — the only prior art is eval_to_f64 in genetic_fitness.rs which only handles constants.
close_reason: Implemented evaluate_constraint() in triage.rs with 52 unit tests. Supports all SymExpr variants, 8 string ops, JS-like semantics.

title: WASM adversarial input generator (proof of concept)
description:
Prove WASM generators work end-to-end across all three frontends (TS, Go, Rust). Content is adversarial/boundary strings — chosen because it doesn't overlap with faker heuristics and is genuinely useful.

Small Rust crate → wasm32-wasip1 via Extism PDK. ~50-100 curated payloads across categories. Recipe selects category; no recipe = random. Prebuilt .wasm checked in.

Categories: sql_injection, xss, unicode_edge, null_bytes, extreme_length, format_string, path_traversal, encoding.

Enhance generate_random_value() in input_gen.rs to use param-name heuristics for TypeInfo::String parameters. Case-insensitive substring match on param name, also check type name/alias. No user config — always active, pure Rust, no external deps.

title: Source-agnostic harvesting: ensure all input sources reach the pool
description: Verify and fix that all execution sources are harvested when they trigger interesting behavior: concolic-solved values, random exploration, fuzzed inputs, user-provided seeds, cross-function pool seeds. The pool should not care about input provenance — a value is interesting because of what it does, not where it came from. Audit current gaps and close them.

title: Harvest from concolic orchestrator: wire pool save in concolic path
description: Bug: orchestrator.rs explore() does not call harvest_from_exploration(). The CLI concolic path loads the pool for seeds but never writes back discovered interesting inputs. Wire harvest_from_exploration() call after concolic exploration completes, and save_pool() in the CLI concolic path. ~30 LoC. Related to str-oi1d (orchestrator modernization).

title: Structure-aware string mutation algorithm
description: A string-specific mutation algorithm distinct from byte-level and existing char-level: multi-point character substitution, regex-pattern-guided insertion, dictionary-weighted character selection, unicode-aware operations. Complements byte-level mutation (for binary) and existing single-char mutation (too granular for structural patterns). Independent.

title: String fragment injection mutation operator
description: New mutation operator in mutate_string: insert a random fragment from the dictionary at a random position in the string. Gets domain-relevant characters like @ and . into strings without waiting for char-by-char luck. This is essentially AFL's dictionary mode applied at the string level. Depends on literal dictionary threading.
close_reason: Already implemented in commit a16927e as part of str-ph9p

title: Thread literal dictionary into mutate_string
description: Add a dictionary: &[&str] parameter to mutate_string() in input_gen.rs. The dictionary contains source-code literals extracted during analysis plus common delimiters (@, ., ://, /, etc.). Currently mutate_string has no access to domain-relevant fragments — it only does random character operations. This is the foundation for fragment injection. ~20 LoC change.

title: Epic: Enhanced mutation operators for strings and binary
description: Strengthen the mutation pipeline with string fragment injection from source literals, substring duplication, havoc mode (compound mutations), structure-aware string mutation, and byte-level mutation for binary buffers. Addresses the gap where the GA's string handling is weakest — character-level perturbation is too sparse for discovering structural patterns like emails or URLs.

title: Parameter drilling: pin non-blocking params, vary blocking parameter
description: When a frontier is stalled, pin non-blocking parameters at their best-known values (from the input that reached the frontier) and apply intensive value-level mutation only to the identified blocking parameter(s). This is a vector-level strategy that delegates to aggressive value-level tactics. Wire into orchestrator exploration loop. Requires frontier set and branch-parameter attribution.
close_reason: Implemented parameter drilling: blocking param identification via SymExpr walk, pin+drill mutation, wired into orchestrator with stall threshold gating. 9 unit tests.

title: Frontier set data structure and tracking
description: Define Frontier struct: { branch_id, depth, blocking_params: Vec<usize>, best_prefix: Vec<Value>, stall_count: u32 }. Maintain a priority queue of frontiers per function during exploration. A frontier is created when a branch is reached but not fully explored. Multiple frontiers can be active simultaneously. New module frontier.rs in shatter-core.

title: Branch-parameter attribution: extract param names from SymExpr constraints
description: Walk SymExpr trees to extract which Param names appear in each branch's symbolic constraint. New function extract_param_names(expr: &SymExpr) -> HashSet<String> in sym_expr.rs or a new attribution module. This tells us which parameters are involved in each branch condition, enabling targeted parameter drilling. Independent — no dependencies.

title: Epic: Multi-frontier exploration with parameter drilling
description: Track multiple frontier branches per function, attribute blocking parameters via symbolic constraint analysis, and apply targeted parameter drilling (pin non-blocking params, intensively mutate the blocking one). Includes stall detection and coverage-guided frontier prioritization. See docs/GLOSSARY.md for terminology.

title: Revalidation CLI command and CI integration
description: Add 'shatter revalidate' CLI command. Text output for terminal showing per-function revalidation results with verdict labels ([CONFIRMED], [REGRESSION], [DRIFT], [FLAKY]). JSON output mode for CI pipelines. Nonzero exit code when any PotentialRegression or SeverityUpgrade verdict is present. Summary statistics at end.

title: Revalidation loop: re-execute pool entries and classify results
description: Load pool entries, re-execute each against current code via frontend, compare observed behavior against recorded behavior. Use fingerprint to detect code changes. Use nondeterminism mask (from nondeterminism epic) to exclude nondeterministic fields from comparison. Disappeared behaviors are potential regressions, NOT garbage for silent eviction. Classify each result using RevalidationVerdict.

title: Revalidation types: RevalidationReport and RevalidationVerdict
description: Define RevalidationReport struct and RevalidationVerdict enum in new revalidation.rs module. Verdicts: Confirmed (behavior unchanged), ExpectedDrift (code fingerprint changed, behavior changed — expected), Flaky (code unchanged, behavior changed — nondeterminism or environment), PotentialRegression (code changed, previously-interesting behavior vanished), SeverityDowngrade (behavior less severe — potential silent fix), SeverityUpgrade (behavior more severe — potential new bug). Each report includes function name, input vector, expected vs observed branch path and severity.

title: Epic: Revalidation and regression detection
description: Re-execute pool entries against current code to detect behavioral regressions. Distinguishes genuine regressions from expected drift (code changed), flaky behavior, and nondeterministic noise. Integrates with nondeterminism mask to avoid false positives. Provides CLI command and CI-ready JSON output with nonzero exit codes.

title: User-declared nondeterminism in .shatter/config.yaml
description:
Add nondeterminism section to ShatterConfig (config.rs) with confirmed and rejected lists. Format:

nondeterminism:
  confirmed:
    - function: createUser
      path: '$.id'
      reason: 'UUID generated per call'
  rejected:
    - function: processOrder
      path: '$.orderId'
      reason: 'deterministic sequence from input'

Confirmed entries become UserDeclared evidence (highest precedence). Rejected entries suppress re-flagging by heuristics so the tool doesn't keep asking about known-deterministic fields.

title: Cache integration for nondeterminism mask
description: Store nondeterminism mask alongside cached behavior map in cache.rs. Detection phase (re-execution sampling) must run BEFORE caching so the mask is available on write. On cache load, the mask is available for comparison without re-detection. When mock responses are constructed from cached behavior maps, nondeterministic fields should be noted — callers tested against a fixed mock UUID may miss bugs.

title: Add nondeterministic_fields to BehaviorMap and FunctionSpec
description: Add Vec<NondeterministicField> field to both BehaviorMap (behavior.rs) and FunctionSpec (spec.rs). Serialize/deserialize with serde. Spec diff (spec_diff.rs) should consult the mask when comparing postconditions — fields listed as nondeterministic are excluded from equality checks regardless of evidence level.

title: Structural similarity scoring for return value comparison
description: JSON tree diff that compares leaf values between two return values, returning a similarity fraction (0.0-1.0). High similarity (>0.9) with one changed field suggests nondeterminism, not regression. Low similarity (<0.5) suggests real behavioral change. Used by revalidation to distinguish minor drift from genuine regressions. Combine with heuristics: structural similarity is the quantitative signal, pattern/name heuristics are the qualitative signal.

title: Name-based heuristics for nondeterminism detection
description: Suffix/substring matching on return value field names: *Id/*ID (0.60 confidence), *UUID (0.95), *Token (0.90), *Nonce (0.90), *_at (0.80), *Timestamp (0.80), *random* (0.85). Confidence-scored NamePattern table. Lower weight when field name matches an input parameter name (likely echo, not generation). Produces NameHeuristic evidence.

title: Value-pattern heuristics for nondeterminism detection
description: Regex and structural matchers for common nondeterministic value patterns: UUID v4, ISO 8601 datetime, Unix timestamps (large integers near current time), random hex strings, JWT tokens, hash strings. Produces PatternMatch evidence. Applied to changed fields during return value comparison.

title: Within-run nondeterminism detection via re-execution sampling
description: Re-execute NONDETERMINISM_SAMPLES_PER_CLASS (default 2) inputs per equivalence class, NONDETERMINISM_REEXECUTION_COUNT (default 3) times each. Compare return values field-by-field using JSON tree diff. Named constants for easy tuning. Three executions distinguishes deterministic (all 3 same), fully nondeterministic (all 3 different), and flaky (2 agree, 1 differs).
close_reason: Re-execution sampling for nondeterminism detection implemented. 12 unit tests, E2E passing. Merge conflicts with str-2wxm resolved.

title: Nondeterminism data model: NondeterministicField and NondeterminismEvidence
description: Create NondeterministicField struct and NondeterminismEvidence enum (UserDeclared, ObservedWithinRun, ObservedAcrossRuns, PatternMatch, NameHeuristic). Asymmetric design: absence from list does NOT assert determinism. New module nondeterminism.rs in shatter-core.

title: Epic: Nondeterminism detection and management
description: Detect, record, and manage nondeterministic return value fields. Asymmetric model: we can prove nondeterminism but never prove determinism. Includes within-run detection, value-pattern and name-based heuristics, structural similarity, BehaviorMap/FunctionSpec integration, cache integration, user-declared config, and interactive review CLI.

title: extension.test.ts uses hardcoded absolute paths
description: shatter-vs/src/test/extension.test.ts has hardcoded absolute paths: line 545 (shatterproofModuleOverride = /home/ketan/...) and line 974 (testfile = /home/ketan/...). These should use path.resolve(__dirname, ...) or similar to derive paths relative to the repo root, so tests work on any machine.
