description: shatter-report/scan-report.json contains absolute paths like /home/ketan/project/shatter/examples/standalone/ts/01-arithmetic.ts. These should be relative paths (e.g. examples/standalone/ts/01-arithmetic.ts) for portability — the report breaks when shared or moved to a different machine. The scan output code that populates file_path needs to relativize paths against the project root.

title: String-level crossover operators for genetic search
description:
The existing crossover_inputs() (str-olf) operates at parameter level and array-element level — it picks whole values from parent A or B. For string parameters, it never combines characters from both parents.

String-level crossover strategies needed:
- Splice crossover: prefix from parent A + suffix from parent B (e.g. 'test@' + 'domain.com' → 'test@domain.com')
- Substring insertion: insert a substring from one parent into the other
- Single-point crossover: split both strings at a random position, swap tails

This naturally solves the literal composition problem (str-omrx context): if parent A is '@' (from a literal seed) and parent B is 'hello' (from boundary), crossover produces 'hel@lo' or '@hello' — strings containing '@' with length > 0.

Should be added to crossover_inputs() in input_gen.rs, gated on the parameter being TypeInfo::Str.

Parent: str-oi1d (genetic search pipeline epic)

title: Replace naive fuzz_inputs with type-aware mutation from input_gen.rs
description: The orchestrator's fuzz_inputs() (orchestrator.rs:467-511) is a hardcoded naive fuzzer: flip bools, ±1 for numbers, empty/'a' for strings. It doesn't know parameter types and can't mutate arrays, objects, unions, or nullable types. Meanwhile input_gen.rs has type-aware mutate_inputs() (line 946) that handles all TypeInfo variants with biased distributions, plus crossover_inputs() (line 1251) for recombining two parent inputs. These are tested but never called from the orchestrator. Replace fuzz_inputs() with mutate_inputs() using the function's ParamInfo from analysis, and add crossover between high-performing parent inputs once fitness scoring is wired in (str-hoyl).
notes: Key insight: fuzz_inputs() is ONLY called for Unknown constraints (orchestrator.rs:428-441) — branches Z3 can't solve. The naive fuzzer is worst exactly where good fuzzing matters most. Opaque/unknown branches are the ones that need type-aware mutation and crossover the most, since they can't be solved symbolically.

title: Wire MetaStrategy into orchestrator as input generation layer
description: The strategy.rs module implements InputStrategy trait and MetaStrategy (adaptive selection with feedback, hit-rate scoring, sliding window) but is never imported by the orchestrator. The orchestrator hardcodes input generation: seeds at startup, Z3 solving for solvable constraints, fuzz_inputs() for unknowns. Replace this with MetaStrategy where each input source (Z3 solver, fuzzer, boundary seeds, random, pool, user-provided) is a registered strategy. After each execution, record_outcome() tells the meta-strategy which source is producing new paths, and feedback() distributes execution results to reactive strategies. This enables the orchestrator to adaptively allocate budget toward whichever generation method is working best for the current function.

title: Wire genetic fitness scoring into orchestrator worklist ordering
description: The orchestrator (orchestrator.rs) uses a BinaryHeap ordered solely by InputSource (UserProvided > Z3Solved > Fuzzed > Seed). Two Z3Solved inputs are treated identically regardless of whether the parent execution reached depth 2 or depth 15. The genetic_fitness module exists with tested scoring logic but is never imported or called outside its own tests. Wire it in: when an execution produces new worklist entries (via Z3 solving or fuzzing), tag those entries with the parent execution's fitness score (including depth). Use fitness-informed ordering so the orchestrator spends its budget on inputs derived from deeper, higher-coverage executions rather than treating all same-source inputs equally.
notes: Design note: orchestrator's covered_paths: HashSet<u64> (line 306) and FitnessContext.seen_paths: HashSet<u64> (genetic_fitness.rs:54) both track path hashes. When wiring fitness in, share state rather than duplicate. Also: WorklistEntry Ord impl (line 139-143) only compares source field — this is the specific code that needs to change to incorporate fitness.

title: Add execution depth axis to genetic fitness function
description: The genetic_fitness module scores executions on 4 axes (coverage, proximity, unknown_bonus, novelty) but none capture how deep into nested control flow an input penetrates. An input reaching branch depth 15 is strictly more valuable than one reaching depth 2 — it's closer to exploring the deepest, hardest-to-reach behaviors. Add a 5th 'depth' component: normalize branch_path.len() via asymptotic formula (len / (len + k)) and add it to FitnessWeights/FitnessBreakdown. This is a prerequisite for wiring fitness into the orchestrator.

title: Symbolic triage: use accumulated constraints to reject uninteresting inputs without execution
description:
When the concolic solver extracts path constraints (e.g. x > 10 && str.contains(s, "@")), we can evaluate those constraints against candidate inputs statically to predict which branch path they would take. If the predicted path is already in seen_paths, discard the input without executing it. This avoids the cost of spawning frontend execution for inputs that are overwhelmingly likely to be redundant.

Implementation sketch:
1. After each execution round, collect the SymConstraint entries from branch_path into a constraint index keyed by branch_id
2. For each candidate input, evaluate known constraints to predict the branch decisions
3. If all predicted branches map to an already-seen path hash, skip execution (fast path)
4. Periodically execute 'predicted-uninteresting' inputs anyway to catch constraint model errors (e.g. 1-in-20 sampling)

Constraints come from BranchDecision.constraint: SymConstraint in explorer.rs. Currently only used for Z3 solving, not for pre-filtering.

Limitations:
- Only works for branches with captured SymConstraints (Unknown constraints can't be evaluated)
- Cannot predict side effects, exceptions, or loop iteration counts
- Need a fallback sampling rate to catch model drift

Even a 50% fast-rejection rate would effectively double throughput of long farming runs.
design: Tristate verdict (Skip/Execute/Indeterminate). Branch trace templates from observed executions. Concrete SymExpr evaluator predicts branch outcomes without frontend IPC. Adaptive self-disabling via skip-rate and misprediction-rate checks. New module: shatter-core/src/triage.rs. Integration point: orchestrator explore() loop before frontend.send(Execute).

title: CLI concolic path doesn't pass literal-derived seeds to orchestrator
description:
The CLI concolic path (main.rs:1121-1149) seeds the orchestrator with only generate_boundary_inputs() + pool + user inputs. The random explorer (explorer.rs:589-632) uses literals_to_candidate_inputs() from the analyze phase, but the concolic path never calls it.

Fix: add literal_candidates = literals_to_candidate_inputs(&func.params, &func.literals) and extend seed_inputs in main.rs after line 1123. This gives the concolic loop access to string literals like '@', '.', 'express' extracted during static analysis.

This is a 2-line fix but secondary to str-6ayh — even with literal seeds, the solver would still produce wrong-type values without the ParamInfo fix.

title: Support time-based exploration limits (--timeout-explore)
description: Currently exploration is bounded by the number of test inputs (--max-inputs). Add a --timeout-explore flag that caps exploration wall-clock time per function. This is more intuitive for users who want to say 'spend 30 seconds per function' rather than guessing an input count. Should work for both random explorer and concolic orchestrator. Note: --timeout-total already exists (parsed but unused) — this is per-function.

title: Implement adaptive MetaStrategy with outcome-based scoring
description: Meta-strategy that selects among registered InputStrategies using adaptive scoring. Core algorithm: each strategy's score is its hit rate (new paths / total candidates) over a sliding window of its last 100 outcomes. Cold-start protection: strategies with fewer than min(20, estimated_size) candidates supplied get the average score of graduated strategies (or 1.0 if all are in cold start), preventing premature deprioritization. Floor: every non-exhausted strategy gets at least 2% allocation (configurable, range 1-5%). When a strategy exhausts, its allocation is redistributed uniformly to all remaining non-exhausted strategies. Selection is weighted random sampling from normalized scores. Config struct: MetaConfig { window_size: 100, cold_start_threshold: 20, floor: 0.02, adaptive: true, static_weights: Option<Vec<(String, f64)>> }. When static_weights is set, uses fixed distribution instead of adaptive (still redistributes uniformly on exhaustion). When adaptive is false and no static_weights, uses pure round-robin.

title: Add strategy configuration to .shatter/config.yaml and CLI flags
description: Allow users to configure the meta-strategy via .shatter/config.yaml and/or CLI flags. Config surface: exploration.adaptive (bool, default true), exploration.score_window (int, default 100), exploration.cold_start (int, default 20), exploration.strategy_floor (float, default 0.02), exploration.strategy_weights (map of strategy name to relative weight, optional). CLI flags: --strategy-weights literals=0.3,random=0.5,boundary=0.2 as shorthand.

title: Unify explorer and orchestrator into single strategy-driven loop
description: Replace the two separate exploration loops (explorer::explore_function with its if-else chain and orchestrator::explore with its BinaryHeap worklist) with a single loop that polls the MetaStrategy. The random explorer path becomes MetaStrategy with [UserProvided, Literals, PoolSeeds, BoundarySeeds, Random]. The concolic explorer path becomes MetaStrategy with [UserProvided, BoundarySeeds, Z3Solver, Fuzzer]. The CLI --concolic flag (or auto-detection of constraint support) selects the strategy set. Single loop: execute → compute path hash → fan out feedback to all strategies → check termination (plateau, budget, all exhausted).

title: Extract random input generation into Random strategy
description: Wrap existing generate_random_inputs() / generate_inputs_with_custom() calls into an InputStrategy implementation. Infinite, feedback is no-op.

title: Extract pool seed input generation into PoolSeeds strategy
description: Wrap the existing pool_seeds iterator logic (lines 351-357 of explorer.rs) into an InputStrategy implementation. Exhaustible, feedback is no-op.

title: Extract user-provided candidate inputs into UserProvided strategy
description: Wrap user-provided candidate inputs (currently only used in orchestrator.rs lines 317-323) into an InputStrategy implementation. Exhaustible, highest base priority. This also fixes str-emw6 (random explorer ignoring --inputs) since the strategy will be available in both explorer paths.

title: Extract fuzz input generation into Fuzzer strategy
description: Strategy that mutates concrete values near unknown constraints. feedback() records inputs that hit Unknown constraints. next() yields from fuzz_inputs() (existing in orchestrator.rs lines 428-441) and mutate()/crossover() from input_gen.rs. Infinite.

title: Extract Z3 constraint solving into Z3Solver strategy
description: Reactive strategy that produces inputs by negating path constraints. feedback() records symbolic constraints from new paths. next() yields from internal queue of solved inputs. Infinite (produces work while unsolved constraints exist), but may have empty queue between feedback cycles. Wraps existing logic from orchestrator.rs lines 395-424.

title: Extract existing literal-derived input generation into Literals strategy
description: Wrap the existing literals_to_candidate_inputs() call and budget logic (lines 343-349 of explorer.rs) into an InputStrategy implementation. Exhaustible, feedback is no-op. Budget capping moves to estimated_size(). Pure refactor — no behavior change.

title: Implement BoundarySeeds strategy with neighbor enumeration
description: Exhaustible strategy that enumerates type-aware boundary values and their neighbors. Int: 0, ±1, ±2, ±3, powers of two (4,8,16,32,64,128,256,1024) with ±1 neighbors, MAX, MAX-1, MIN, MIN+1 (~36 values per param). Float: 0.0, ±1.0, ±0.5, ±2.0, π, -π, e, -e, π/2, π/4, 0.1, 0.3 (IEEE 754 trap), ±1e-10, ±1e10, NaN, ±Inf, MIN_POSITIVE, MAX, (1<<53) precision boundary. String: empty, space, single char, two chars, long string (300 chars), tab, newline, CRLF, null byte, embedded null, ANSI escape, multibyte unicode (é, 日本語, 🎉), stringified primitives (true, false, null, 0, -1), injection canaries. Bool: true, false. For multi-param functions, use pairwise coverage (not full cartesian product) — vary one param while holding others at neutral defaults (0, empty string, false), then rotate. This caps candidates at ~36*N instead of 36^N.
close_reason: BoundarySeeds strategy with expanded boundary_dict (int ~35, float ~29, string ~21 values with neighbor enumeration) merged to main

title: Define InputStrategy trait and StrategyContext struct
description: Create the core trait that all input strategies implement. Interface: next(&mut self, ctx: &StrategyContext) -> Option<Vec<Value>> to produce candidates, feedback(&mut self, result: &ExecuteResult, was_new_path: bool) for outcome notification, name(&self) -> &str for attribution, estimated_size(&self) -> Option<u64> for exhaustible strategies (None = infinite). StrategyContext holds param types, analysis metadata (literals, branch conditions), and frontend capabilities. Place in a new shatter-core/src/strategy.rs module.
close_reason: InputStrategy trait, StrategyContext, MetaStrategy with adaptive scoring, and 13 tests implemented in strategy.rs

title: Input strategy decomposition and adaptive meta-strategy
description: Replace the ad-hoc if-else input generation chain in explorer.rs and the separate worklist model in orchestrator.rs with a unified InputStrategy trait and adaptive meta-strategy. Each input source becomes a composable strategy struct with a next()/feedback() interface. A meta-strategy selects among strategies using outcome-based adaptive scoring with configurable cold-start protection, per-strategy floor allocation, and optional user-specified static weights. This unifies the two explorer paths under a single loop with different strategy sets.

title: Add string method examples to walkthrough demo
description:
The walkthrough script (demo/walkthrough.sh) exercises arithmetic, control-flow, and error-throwing functions, but no string-method functions. Add a step that runs 'shatter explore' on a string-heavy standalone example (e.g. 15-email-validator.ts or a simpler string-switch function) and validates output. This ensures the string ops → Z3 pipeline stays healthy across releases.

Suggested insertion point: after the existing single-function explore steps, before the scan steps. Should exercise at least one function that uses indexOf, startsWith, endsWith, or includes.

title: Scan mode does not use config-loaded candidate inputs
description: scan_orchestrator.rs only converts pool seeds via pool_to_candidate_inputs(). It never reads FunctionConfig.candidate_inputs from .shatter/config.yaml or --inputs. Users who provide per-function candidate inputs in their project config get no benefit during scan. The fix should mirror how the concolic path in main.rs:1094-1097 converts CandidateInput to Vec<Vec<Value>> and passes them to the explorer.

title: Concolic explorer stuck on single path when initial execution yields no constraints
description: When the concolic explorer's first execution returns empty path_constraints (e.g. validateEmail('') hits 'email.length === 0' and returns immediately with zero branch decisions), the solver has nothing to negate and exploration terminates after one iteration. The orchestrator should fall back to using static branch conditions from the analyze phase as a constraint skeleton, or use literal/boundary seeds to reach deeper code paths before starting the concolic loop. Currently it tries boundary value seeds (empty string, 0, true, etc.) but none of these contain '@' so they all hit the same first two branches. The concolic explorer needs a seeding strategy that uses structural information from the analyzed branches (e.g. literals like '@' appearing in indexOf conditions) to construct inputs that reach past guard clauses.
notes: Reproduced in e2e_concolic.rs::concolic_validateemail_boundary_seeds_only. Root cause: solver declares string params as Int (str-6ayh). Secondary: concolic path missing literal seeds (str-bb5a). Related improvements: destructuring (str-90ws), closure mutable capture (str-mu3h).

title: Inline callback scope detection
description: Handle arrow functions and function expressions passed as arguments to higher-order functions (e.g. items.map(x => x > 0 ? x*2 : 0)). When the instrumentor encounters a function expression used as a call argument, wrap it with CallEnter/CallExit scope markers so each invocation is recognized as a separate scope by the core's path hashing. Without this, branches inside inline callbacks repeat in the trace without scope boundaries, causing the same path explosion as untagged loops. TS: detect ArrowFunction/FunctionExpression in call argument positions. Go: detect function literals in call argument positions. This is the hardest case because the 'function' isn't a standalone declaration — it's an expression inline at the call site.

title: Scope-aware path hashing in explorer
description: Replace the current path_hash() in explorer.rs which hashes every (branch_id, taken) pair in sequence. New algorithm: parse the branch trace as a tree of scopes using LoopEnter/LoopExit markers, then for each loop scope that repeats, collapse to the set of unique (branch_id, taken) pairs seen across all iterations. Preserve ordering between scopes at the same nesting level. Hash the collapsed structure. This fixes the path explosion where e.g. intToRoman reports 30 distinct paths because different loop iteration counts produce different hashes. Expected result: intToRoman collapses to ~16 meaningful paths (one per roman numeral tier + error + edge cases). Must handle: nested loops (each loop_id collapses independently), early exits (break/return mid-loop — incomplete iteration still captured in the set), loops with internal branching (different branch sets across iterations remain distinct paths). Falls back to current behavior when no scope markers present (backward compat with old frontends).

title: Loop scope markers in frontend instrumentation
description: Add LoopEnter(loop_id)/LoopExit(loop_id) scope events to the branch trace emitted by frontends. Both TS and Go instrumentors already know when they're processing loop AST nodes (while, for, do-while, for-of, for-in, range). Emit scope markers around loop bodies so the core can identify which branch decisions belong to which loop iteration. Each loop gets a unique loop_id per function. Nested loops get distinct IDs. For iterator-style loops (for-of, for-in, range) that lack an explicit boolean condition, still emit scope markers around the body. Protocol: add ScopeEvent variant (or parallel trace field) to carry LoopEnter/LoopExit alongside BranchDecision in ExecuteResult.

title: Call scope markers for recursion and callback tracking
description: Add CallEnter(call_site_id)/CallExit(call_site_id) scope events emitted by instrumented functions. Each instrumented function emits scope-enter at entry and scope-exit at return/throw. This handles: (1) Recursion — recursive calls produce nested CallEnter/CallExit, allowing path_hash to collapse repeated recursive frames the same way it collapses loop iterations. (2) Functional callbacks — map/filter/reduce/forEach invoke callbacks multiple times; if the callback is an instrumented function, its enter/exit markers let the core collapse repetitions. (3) Recursion containing loops containing recursion — scope markers nest arbitrarily and each scope level collapses independently. Implementation: inject scope-enter preamble and scope-exit at all return points (or use try/finally equivalent) in every instrumented function. No need to instrument call sites — only callees. Protocol: extend ScopeEvent to include CallEnter/CallExit variants.

title: Render scan/explore output as rich terminal report by default
description: Replace raw JSON/text scan and explore output with rich terminal rendering using termimad. When stdout is a TTY, render Markdown output with colors, tables, and formatting via termimad (crossterm-based). When piped or redirected, emit plain Markdown (no ANSI escapes). Use std::io::stdout().is_terminal() for detection. This makes pretty output the default with zero flags. Add termimad as a dependency of shatter-cli. Depends on str-3k35 (Markdown report generation) since we need Markdown content to render.

This includes behavior maps, coverage metrics, cluster info, and per-function summaries. This is a prerequisite for str-wtxu (HTML report) since the HTML report can be generated by converting the Markdown output to HTML rather than building a separate HTML template system.

title: Walkthrough: ship v1/v2 fixture pair for meaningful spec-diff demo
description:
## Problem

Step 23 of the walkthrough generates a spec, copies it as both old and new, then diffs identical files — always showing 'No changes detected'. This makes the spec-diff feature look like a no-op.

## Solution

Ship two versions of the same function as demo fixtures:
- `demo/fixtures/arithmetic-v1.ts` — baseline (matches current 01-arithmetic.ts behavior)
- `demo/fixtures/arithmetic-v2.ts` — intentional behavioral change (e.g., added branch, changed boundary, removed a case)

Update step 23 to explore both fixtures and diff the resulting specs, producing real output showing added/removed/changed behaviors.

## Design Considerations

### Fixture design
The v2 file should have a small but meaningful change — ideally one that adds a branch and modifies a boundary so the diff output demonstrates both 'added behavior' and 'changed behavior' sections. Keep both files short and well-commented explaining what changed and why.

### Maintenance
Both fixtures must be updated if the spec JSON format changes. Add a CI check or walkthrough gate that catches format drift.

### Broader use case: code similarity / duplication detection
This fixture pair also opens a second use case for spec-diff beyond regression testing: **comparing two independent implementations to determine behavioral similarity**. If two functions have the same signature but were written separately (copy-paste drift, parallel implementations, candidate refactors), spec-diff can reveal whether they are behaviorally equivalent, partially overlapping, or divergent.

This is distinct from regression testing (same code, different points in time) — it's cross-implementation comparison (different code, same point in time). The spec-diff machinery already supports this since it compares behavioral specs regardless of how they were generated. Worth considering:

1. **Walkthrough addition**: A second spec-diff step showing 'compare two implementations' as a separate use case (e.g., diff specs of a function and its refactored version).
2. **CLI UX**: Currently spec-diff takes two JSON files. A convenience form like `shatter compare func1.ts::add func2.ts::add` could generate-and-diff in one step.
3. **Similarity scoring**: The diff currently shows structural changes. A summary metric (e.g., '3 of 5 behaviors match — 60% equivalent') would make the duplication detection use case more actionable.

These extensions are out of scope for this issue but worth tracking separately if the use case proves valuable.

title: Walkthrough: default to auto mode, opt-in to interactive
description: Currently the walkthrough defaults to interactive mode (pauses after each step). This is backwards for the common case — CI and quick verification want auto mode, interactive is the exception. Flip the default so the walkthrough runs continuously unless --interactive is passed. The --auto flag becomes a no-op or is removed.

title: Integrate crypto registry (Layer 1) into frontends and add protocol message
description:
Extract from str-mxa3: wire the existing crypto registry (str-34xq) into frontend analysis pipelines and define the protocol message for reporting detected crypto boundaries to core.

1. Define a CryptoBoundary protocol message (or add to existing analyze response) that frontends use to report matched crypto calls
2. TS frontend: load crypto-registry.toml at startup, match call targets during analysis, report via protocol
3. Go frontend: same integration
4. Rust frontend: same integration (or stub if analyze is too early)
5. Integration test: analyze a file that calls crypto.createDecipheriv, verify the boundary is reported

This is the 'end-to-end validation' that str-9asc and str-tycq were waiting for. Once this works, Layers 2 and 3 can integrate using the same protocol message format.

title: Improve shatter explore output readability with color, layout, and visual hierarchy
description:
The current explore output is functional but visually flat and hard to scan. It needs:

**Current problems:**
- No color — everything is monochrome terminal text
- No visual separation between functions — just a blank line
- Path clusters blend together with no visual anchoring
- Coverage metrics are buried at the bottom with no visual weight
- Long argument strings (e.g. JSON objects) get truncated mid-line awkwardly
- No summary header or footer for multi-function runs

**Proposed improvements:**

1. **Color coding** (via ANSI escape codes, respecting NO_COLOR/TERM):
   - Function names in bold/bright
   - Return values in green, thrown errors in red/yellow
   - Coverage percentages: green ≥80%, yellow 50-79%, red <50%
   - Cluster numbers in cyan/bold
   - Dim/gray for less important details (constraint counts)

2. **Layout & structure**:
   - Box-drawing or rule lines between functions (e.g. ─── or ═══)
   - Indented tree-style for path clusters with │ ├ └ connectors
   - Summary banner at top: 'Exploring 4 functions...' and footer with totals
   - Coverage bar: visual [████░░░░] 58% instead of just text

3. **ASCII art / visual elements**:
   - Mini progress/coverage bars per function
   - Status icons: ✓ for good coverage, ⚠ for partial, ✗ for low
   - Error paths get a distinct marker (⚡ or 💥 or red ✗)

4. **Argument formatting**:
   - Truncate long JSON args intelligently (show key structure, elide values)
   - Align example calls for visual scanning

5. **Respect terminal conventions**:
   - NO_COLOR env var disables all ANSI codes
   - --no-color CLI flag
   - Detect non-TTY (piped output) and auto-disable color
   - --color=always/auto/never flag

**Reference output (example of what it could look like):**
```
═══ Shatter Explore ═══  4 functions

── classifyNumber (01-arithmetic.ts:10) ──────────────
  4 paths · 7/12 lines (58%) [████▌···] ⚠

  ├─ 1. returns "zero"
  │     classifyNumber(0.0) → "zero"
  ├─ 2. returns "positive-even"
  │     classifyNumber(2.0) → "positive-even"
  ├─ 3. returns "positive-odd"
  │     classifyNumber(0.5) → "positive-odd"
  └─ 4. returns "negative"
        classifyNumber(-23.0) → "negative"

  Branches: 3/3 (100%) ✓  Constraints: 203/203 (100%) ✓

── safeDivide (04-errors.ts:10) ──────────────────────
  3 paths · 7/13 lines (54%) [████····] ⚠

  ├─ 1. ⚡ throws Error: division by zero [validation]
  │     safeDivide(0.0, 0.0) → Error
  ├─ 2. returns -0.0294...
  │     safeDivide(1.0, -34.0) → -0.0294
  └─ 3. returns 0
        safeDivide(0.0, 0.5) → 0

  Branches: 3/3 (100%) ✓  Constraints: 278/278 (100%) ✓

═══ Summary: 15 paths across 4 functions ═══
```

**Implementation location:** `format_exploration_report()` in `shatter-core/src/explorer.rs` and the CLI display code in `shatter-cli/src/main.rs`.

Consider using the `owo-colors` or `colored` crate for ANSI color support, and `unicode-width` for proper terminal width handling.

title: Add fs2-based flock locking for .shatter/ shared state files
description:
## Problem

When multiple shatter processes run concurrently (e.g. make -j4 with per-file explore targets), shared state files in .shatter/ are subject to lost-update races. Atomic writes (temp+rename) prevent corruption but not lost updates — two processes can read pool.json, add entries, and last-rename-wins loses the first process's additions.

Affected files: .shatter/seeds/pool.json, .shatter/batch-state.json, any future shared mutable state.

## Solution

Use the fs2 crate (thin wrapper over flock() syscall) for exclusive locking around read-modify-write operations on shared state files. flock is kernel-managed — locks release automatically on process exit, crash, SIGKILL, or OOM. No stale locks possible, no expiration needed.

Pattern:
```rust
use fs2::FileExt;
let lock = File::create(".shatter/pool.lock")?;
lock.lock_exclusive()?;
// read-modify-write pool.json
// lock released when dropped
```

For non-critical paths (e.g. pool update after exploration), use try_lock_exclusive() and skip the update if another process holds the lock — pool updates are best-effort.

## Scope

1. Add fs2 dependency to shatter-core/Cargo.toml
2. Create a small locking helper in shatter-core (e.g. src/file_lock.rs) wrapping fs2 with .shatter/*.lock convention
3. Wrap InterestingPool save/load in exclusive lock
4. Wrap BatchState save/load in exclusive lock
5. Add integration test verifying concurrent access doesn't lose updates

## Not in scope
- SQLite migration (separate decision if needed later)
- Per-file pool splitting (orthogonal optimization)

title: Add --project-dir CLI flag for explicit project root override
description: Add a --project-dir <path> flag to the CLI that overrides auto-detected project root. Needed for monorepos and workspace layouts where the auto-detection heuristic picks the wrong root. Pass the value through to frontends the same way as auto-detected project root. If both auto-detection and --project-dir are available, --project-dir wins.

title: Project root detection and environment setup for frontends
description: Auto-detect the project root by walking up from the target file looking for package.json / go.mod / tsconfig.json. Pass project root to frontends via protocol or env var. TS frontend: load the project's tsconfig.json (path aliases, baseUrl, jsx settings), set NODE_PATH to the project's node_modules/. Go frontend: copy both go.mod AND go.sum into the temp build dir. Detection happens in shatter-core and is communicated to frontends via the setup or analyze protocol message. Single isolated source files with no project structure must continue to work — only activate project-awareness when a project root is actually found.
design:
## Design

**Detection (shatter-core):** Add detect_project_root(file_path) -> Option<ProjectRoot> that walks up from the target file looking for markers in priority order: package.json, tsconfig.json, go.mod, Cargo.toml. Returns ProjectRoot { path, kind }. None for isolated files.

**Communication — protocol field:** Add optional project_root: Option<String> to Analyze, Instrument, and Setup commands. Per-request, visible in protocol logs, documented in schema.

**TS frontend:** When project_root present: load tsconfig.json via ts.readConfigFile(), set NODE_PATH to project_root/node_modules for createRequire() resolution.

**Go frontend:** When project_root present: use directly instead of walk-up, copy both go.mod AND go.sum, run go mod download if external deps detected.

**Rust frontend:** No changes (execute unimplemented).

**Isolated files:** project_root=None → exact current behavior, no regression.

### File changes
- shatter-core/src/protocol.rs — Add project_root to Analyze, Instrument, Setup
- shatter-core/src/project.rs (new) — detect_project_root(), ProjectRoot, ProjectKind
- shatter-core/src/explorer.rs — Call detect_project_root(), pass to frontend requests
- shatter-core/src/scan_orchestrator.rs — Detect once per scan, reuse
- shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts — Load tsconfig.json when project_root provided
- shatter-ts/src/executor.ts — Set NODE_PATH for project_root
- shatter-ts/src/protocol.ts — Add project_root to request types
- shatter-go/instrument/instrument.go — Use project_root for go.mod/go.sum
- shatter-go/protocol/handler.go — Pass project_root through
- protocol/schemas/*.json — Update schemas

### Test plan
1. Unit: detect_project_root() on examples/typescript/ → package.json
2. Unit: detect_project_root() on examples/go/ → go.mod
3. Unit: detect_project_root() on temp file with no markers → None
4. Integration: analyze examples/typescript/src/12-external-deps.ts with project root
5. Integration: analyze standalone .ts file → no regression
6. Protocol round-trip: Analyze with/without project_root

title: Extract magic literals to named constants

title: Research: React hooks requiring special concolic support
description: Investigate whether React hooks like useState, useEffect, useMemo, useReducer, and useContext need special handling in the concolic engine. Key questions: (1) Does useState's setter-as-callback pattern need special symbolic treatment? (2) Do hooks' ordering rules (Rules of Hooks) create analysis constraints? (3) Can useEffect callbacks be explored as separate entry points? (4) Does conditional hook usage (which React forbids but code may contain) need detection? (5) Do custom hooks compose in ways that need unwrapping? Document findings and recommend which hooks need engine-level support vs. which work with standard analysis.
close_reason: Doing research directly in conversation instead

title: Support React code analysis
description: Add support for analyzing React components and patterns. React code has distinct control flow patterns: conditional rendering (ternary in JSX, && short-circuit), component lifecycle, event handlers, and hooks-driven state. The analyzer needs to understand these patterns to explore branches effectively. Depends on TSX file support.
design:
## Design

No engine or protocol changes needed. React components are plain functions.

**React shim module (shatter-ts/src/react-shim.ts):** Lightweight mock of react and react/jsx-runtime:
- jsx/jsxs/createElement: return plain {type, props, key} objects
- useState(init): returns [init_value, noop]. Evaluates init if function.
- useReducer(reducer, init): returns [init, noop]
- useEffect/useLayoutEffect: noop (skip side effects)
- useMemo(factory): calls factory() (may contain branches)
- useCallback(fn): returns fn
- useRef(init): returns {current: init}
- useContext(ctx): returns ctx._currentValue (React default)
- useId(): returns static ':r0:'

**Injection:** Wrap sandbox require in executor.ts — intercept require('react'), require('react/jsx-runtime'), require('react/jsx-dev-runtime') and return shim. Only for .tsx files. Non-React code unaffected.

**What works:** Component execution, conditional rendering branches, useState initializers, useMemo factories with branches.
**What's intentionally skipped:** useEffect callbacks, state updates, re-renders. useContext returns default (custom values via setup/generators).

### File changes
- shatter-ts/src/react-shim.ts (new) — React + jsx-runtime mocks
- shatter-ts/src/executor.ts — Wrap require with React-aware interception for .tsx
- shatter-ts/src/executor.test.ts — Tests for React component execution
- examples/typescript/src/ (new fixture) — Example React component with conditional rendering

### Test plan
1. Unit: useState(0) → [0, noop], useState(() => 42) → [42, noop]
2. Unit: jsx('div', {children: 'hello'}) → element object
3. Integration: Execute component with conditional rendering, verify branch decisions
4. Integration: Component with useMemo branches → branches explored
5. Integration: Non-React .ts file → no shim, no change
6. Negative: useEffect callback with throw → doesn't execute

### Independence from str-guab
Shim intercepts before require resolution — works without project root detection. Can be parallelized.
notes: Research conclusion (2026-03-03): React hooks need NO engine/protocol changes. Components are plain functions (props→JSX). Hooks should be stubbed via a mock React runtime injected into the executor VM sandbox (useState returns [init, noop], useEffect is noop, useMemo calls factory, etc.). JSX conditional expressions (&&, ternary) are already standard AST nodes the instrumentor handles. Only real work: (1) React mock shim in executor sandbox, (2) useContext value injection via setup/custom generators.
close_reason: React shim with mock hooks and jsx-runtime, injected via require interception for .tsx files, 7 new tests, 230 total passing

title: Support TSX file analysis
description: The TypeScript frontend currently handles .ts files but needs to support .tsx files (TypeScript with JSX syntax). This requires: parsing JSX/TSX syntax in the analyzer, handling JSX expressions as branches/paths, and ensuring the executor can run TSX code (may need esbuild/swc transform step). TSX is widely used in React projects so this is a prerequisite for React ecosystem support.
close_reason: TSX support via ScriptKind detection + jsx compiler option, 10 tests, no new deps

title: Wire pool into scan_orchestrator and CLI
description: Integration: (1) scan_orchestrator.rs: add pool: Option<Arc<InterestingPool>> to ScanConfig. Load from .shatter/seeds/pool.json at scan start. Pass to each explore_function call. Write back after all functions complete. Pool grows during scan — functions explored later in topological order benefit from earlier discoveries. (2) CLI: add --seeds-dir flag to explore and scan commands (defaults to .shatter/seeds/). Add --no-seeds flag to disable pool loading/saving. (3) Single-function explore mode: load pool, use as seeds, update pool with discoveries, write back. (4) Update .gitignore guidance: .shatter/cache/ should be ignored, .shatter/seeds/ should be committed.

title: Consume pool as seeds during exploration
description: When starting exploration of a function, inject matching pool values as seed inputs. Steps: (1) Load pool once at scan start (scan_orchestrator holds Arc<InterestingPool> alongside existing Arc<BehaviorMapCache>). (2) For each function, for each parameter, find pool entries where pool_entry.type == param.typ. (3) For each match, construct single-value input vector: matching value at that param position, boundary defaults at other positions — same approach as literals_to_candidate_inputs in input_gen.rs. (4) In concolic mode (orchestrator.rs): inject as Seed priority worklist entries. In random mode (explorer.rs): prepend to iteration queue after literal candidates but before random generation. (5) Track which pool values triggered novel paths during this exploration to update hit_count/last_hit_epoch.

title: Populate pool from exploration results
description: After each function's exploration completes (in both explorer.rs random loop and orchestrator.rs concolic loop), harvest interesting inputs into the pool. Steps: (1) Iterate new_path_executions from ExplorationResult. (2) Classify each as error-triggering (use severity classifier) or rare-path (exercised by ≤2 inputs). (3) Decompose input vectors into individual (value, TypeInfo) pairs using the function's param types. Also store the full compound input if it's an Object/Array type. (4) Skip values already in boundary_dict (0, -1, empty string, etc. are tried everywhere already). (5) For existing pool entries matching (type, value), append new BehaviorObservation and update last_hit_epoch — this handles the case where a middling-interest value becomes highly interesting for a different function. (6) For new entries, insert and run eviction if bucket exceeds cap.

title: Implement coverage-tier scoring and eviction
description: Implement the two-tier eviction algorithm for the interesting input pool. Per-type bucket cap (default 50, configurable). Coverage tier: tier 1 = entry is sole witness to at least one BehaviorSig; tier 0 = all behaviors have other witnesses. Eviction priority: always evict tier-0 first (lowest quality = severity × breadth where breadth = log2(1 + distinct_function_count)). If all tier-1: evict entry whose unique behaviors have lowest max severity (sacrifice least-severe unique coverage). On eviction, recheck remaining entries — an entry may promote from tier 0 to tier 1 if the evicted entry was the other witness. Build HashMap<BehaviorSig, Vec<EntryIndex>> per bucket for O(1) lookups. Recompute tiers on every insertion (cheap at bucket size ≤50).

title: Classify error severity from ExecuteResult
description: Implement severity classification for execution results. Tiering: (1) Crash (severity=4): frontend process died, timeout, pipe closed, or protocol error. (2) UnhandledError (severity=3): thrown_error with runtime error type names — TypeError, ReferenceError, RangeError, NullPointerException, panic, etc. Build a list of known runtime error type patterns per language. (3) HandledError (severity=2): thrown_error with application-defined exception types (anything not matching the runtime error list). (4) RarePath (severity=1): no error, but path was novel. Input: ExecuteResult + path novelty flag. Output: Severity enum. Place in interesting_pool.rs or a classify_severity() function.

title: Implement pool persistence to .shatter/seeds/pool.json
description: Load and save the interesting input pool to disk at .shatter/seeds/pool.json. Follow the same patterns as BehaviorMapCache in cache.rs: atomic write via temp+rename, create dirs on first write, return Ok(None) for missing file. Single file, not per-function. Use canonical JSON serialization with sorted keys for deterministic output. Deterministic entry ordering: type (canonical string) → coverage_tier desc → quality desc → canonical JSON value. Pool file has top-level version (2) and epoch (monotonic counter incremented each scan). The .shatter/seeds/ directory is designed to be git-committed (unlike .shatter/cache/ which is gitignored).

title: Add error_category field to ErrorInfo protocol type (option 3)
description:
Extend ErrorInfo with a structured error_category enum so frontends classify errors at capture time using language-level signals.

Proposed enum values: validation, runtime, infrastructure, unknown

Frontend classification heuristics:
- TS: custom error subclasses or names matching *Validation*/*Invalid* → validation. TypeError/ReferenceError/SyntaxError → runtime. Timeout → infrastructure.
- Go: known panic types and timeout → infrastructure. Custom error types implementing specific interfaces → validation. Generic panics → runtime.
- Rust: similar mapping from error type hierarchies.

Also classify based on caught/uncaught status (caught errors more likely intentional) and stack depth (shallow throws in the target function more likely validation than deep library errors).

Implementation scope:
1. Add error_category: Option<String> to ErrorInfo in shatter-core/src/protocol.rs and execution_record.rs
2. Update protocol JSON schemas in protocol/schemas/
3. Implement classification in each frontend (TS executor.ts, Go executor.go, Rust handler.rs)
4. Add round-trip serialization tests for the new field
5. Update exploration report to surface the category

Complements but does not depend on branch-path correlation (option 2). The frontend does NOT have branch context — classification is purely from error type, caught/uncaught status, and stack depth.

title: Classify error intent via branch-path correlation (option 2)
description:
Infer whether a thrown error is a deliberate validation rejection or an accidental runtime failure by correlating ExecuteResult.thrown_error with the immediately preceding BranchDecision entries in branch_path.

Pattern to detect: a guard branch (if condition) followed immediately by a throw with no intervening branches suggests intentional input rejection. Accidental errors (null deref, type mismatch) typically occur mid-expression without a preceding guard.

Implementation scope:
- Add classification logic in shatter-core, post-execution, operating on existing ExecuteResult fields (branch_path + thrown_error)
- Output a confidence-scored label (e.g. likely_validation, likely_runtime, unknown)
- No protocol changes required — works on existing data
- Add to exploration report so users can see which error paths are deliberate vs accidental

Complements but does not depend on frontend-level error classification (option 3).

title: Crypto API registry for known library functions
description:
Ship a static config file mapping known crypto function paths that frontends consult during analysis. This is Layer 1 of crypto function recognition (str-mxa3) — zero runtime cost, high precision.

Registry format: TOML or JSON, organized by language and package. Each entry specifies:
- Function path (e.g. crypto.createDecipheriv)
- Direction: encrypt | decrypt | both (for authenticated encryption)
- Parameter roles: which arg is key, which is data, which is IV/nonce
- Output semantics: plaintext or ciphertext

Initial coverage:
- Node.js: crypto module (createDecipheriv, privateDecrypt, subtle.decrypt), crypto-js (CryptoJS.AES.decrypt, CryptoJS.DES.decrypt, etc.), tweetnacl (secretbox.open), libsodium-wrappers
- Go: crypto/aes + crypto/cipher (NewCBCDecrypter, AEAD.Open/Seal, Stream XORKeyStream), x/crypto (chacha20poly1305, nacl/secretbox)
- Rust: aes-gcm (Aes256Gcm::decrypt), ring (aead::open_in_place, aead::seal_in_place), chacha20poly1305 crate

Frontends load the registry at startup and match against call targets during analysis/instrumentation. Matched calls get reported to the core engine via the crypto boundary protocol message.

The registry file lives in a well-known location (e.g. shatter-core/data/crypto-registry.toml) and is extensible by users via a local override file.
close_reason: Crypto registry with 50+ entries, include_str! embedding, HashMap lookup, override support

title: Crypto function recognition for language frontends
description:
Frontends need to detect when a function call involves encryption/decryption so they can trigger crypto boundary splitting (str-mka). This is the detection layer that feeds into boundary splitting.

Recognition strategies (layered, from cheapest to most expensive):

**Layer 1: Known API signatures (static)**
Match against a curated registry of crypto function names/paths:
- Node.js: crypto.createDecipheriv, crypto.createDecipheriv, crypto.privateDecrypt, subtle.decrypt
- npm packages: crypto-js (CryptoJS.AES.decrypt, etc.), tweetnacl (nacl.secretbox.open), libsodium-wrappers
- Go: crypto/aes, crypto/cipher (cipher.Stream, cipher.AEAD .Open/.Seal), x/crypto
- Rust: aes/aes-gcm crate decrypt methods, ring::aead::open_in_place

Registry is a config file shipped with Shatter, updatable without code changes.

**Layer 2: Naming heuristic (static)**  
Flag functions whose names match patterns: decrypt*, decipher*, *_decrypt, *_decipher, unseal, open (in crypto context). High recall, some false positives — use as candidate list for Layer 3 confirmation.

**Layer 3: Behavioral/entropy detection (dynamic)**
At runtime, compare input/output entropy of suspected functions. Decryption characteristically transforms high-entropy input (ciphertext) into lower-entropy output (plaintext). Measure Shannon entropy of input vs output bytes — a significant entropy drop flags likely decryption. Also detect the inverse (encryption: low → high entropy).

**Layer 4: Taint flow patterns (dynamic)**
A function that takes (key, data) → output where the key is short/fixed and data is variable, and the output length ≈ input length, fits the crypto profile. Combine with entropy analysis for high confidence.

Deliverables:
- Crypto function registry format (TOML/JSON config)
- Initial registry for Node.js, Go, Rust standard libs + top packages
- Runtime entropy analyzer utility
- Frontend protocol message to report detected crypto boundaries to core

title: Crypto boundary splitting for concolic execution
description:
When a function decrypts input before branching on the decrypted contents, the solver faces a symbolic barrier — crypto operations produce intractable constraints. Boundary splitting addresses this by intercepting at the decryption call boundary:

1. Detect calls to known decrypt functions (see beads-XXX for recognition)
2. Capture the key (from parameter or constant extraction) and ciphertext input
3. Treat the decrypted plaintext as a fresh symbolic variable
4. Solve constraints on the plaintext domain (normal concolic execution)
5. Re-encrypt solved plaintext values using the captured key to produce valid ciphertext inputs

Requires: crypto function recognition, key capture, encrypt oracle construction (calling the inverse function), and a protocol extension so frontends can report crypto boundaries to the core engine.

Scope: Start with the protocol extension and TypeScript frontend (Node.js crypto module + popular npm packages like crypto-js). Go and Rust frontends follow the same pattern.

title: Add complex realistic example code that challenges the explorer
description:
## Problem
Current examples are trivially simple functions (classifyNumber, classifyString, etc.) that random guessing can easily cover. They don't demonstrate shatter's value over naive fuzzing, and they don't exercise edge cases like opaque types, complex control flow, or real-world patterns.

## What to add

### 1. Complex control flow examples
- Functions with nested conditionals (4+ levels deep)
- State machines with multi-step transitions
- Functions where branch conditions depend on computed values (not direct parameter comparison)
- Regex-based dispatch (e.g., route matching, input validation)
- Functions with early returns creating asymmetric path depths

### 2. Type-challenge examples
- Functions accepting types shatter doesn't know how to handle (database connections, file handles, streams, sockets)
- Functions mixing opaque and primitive params (e.g., `processRequest(socket: net.Socket, timeout: number)`)
- Generic/template functions where type parameters affect behavior
- Union types with many variants
- Deeply nested object types

### 3. Real-world patterns
- Authentication/authorization logic with token validation
- HTTP request routing with middleware chains
- Data transformation pipelines (parse → validate → transform → serialize)
- Rate limiting with time-dependent behavior
- Configuration merging with defaults and overrides

### 4. Adapted open-source code (with attribution)
Source candidates (all MIT/Apache-2.0 licensed):
- lodash utility functions with complex type handling
- Express.js middleware patterns
- Zod schema validation logic
- date-fns date manipulation with edge cases
- path-to-regexp URL pattern matching

Each adapted function should:
- Preserve the original logic complexity
- Include attribution comment with source repo and license
- Document expected branches and why random guessing fails
- Have a companion .shatter/config.yaml showing recommended configuration

### 5. Opaque type examples
- Functions that SHOULD be skipped (net.Socket, fs.ReadStream parameters)
- Functions that SHOULD NOT be skipped (using custom types that look opaque but aren't)
- Functions mixing executable and non-executable params

## Acceptance criteria
- At least 5 new example functions per language (TS, Go)
- Each has documented expected branch count and difficulty
- Random input generation covers <50% of branches (demonstrating need for solver)
- Examples are organized by difficulty: easy/, medium/, hard/
- Walkthrough updated to include at least one complex example

title: Cache analysis results with file hash validation
description:
## Problem
Every shatter run re-analyzes source files from scratch, even when files haven't changed. For large codebases this wastes time on the most expensive phase (AST parsing + type inference).

## Proposed solution
Cache analysis results (FunctionAnalysis structs) keyed by file path, with staleness detection:

### Cache key strategy
- **File-level cache**: Store analysis per source file, keyed by `(file_path, content_hash)`
- **Content hash**: SHA-256 of file contents (fast, reliable, ignores timestamps)
- **Timestamp fast-path**: Compare mtime first — if unchanged, skip hashing. If mtime differs, hash to confirm actual change (handles touch, save-without-change, etc.)

### File-level vs function-level
File-level is the right granularity because:
1. Analysis operates on whole files (the frontend parses the entire AST)
2. A change anywhere in the file can affect any function (imports, shared constants, type changes)
3. Function-level would require tracking cross-function dependencies within a file, adding complexity with little benefit
4. File-level cache entries are small (just the FunctionAnalysis vec)

### Storage
- Location: `.shatter/cache/analysis/` (separate from behavior map cache)
- Format: `<file_path_hash>.json` containing `{ content_hash, mtime, analyses: FunctionAnalysis[] }`
- On cache hit: return cached analyses, skip the Analyze protocol command entirely
- On cache miss: analyze via frontend, store result

### Invalidation
- Content hash mismatch → re-analyze
- Manual: `shatter cache clear --analysis`
- Analysis cache entries include the protocol version — version bump invalidates all

## Tests
- Cache hit: same file content → returns cached analysis, no frontend call
- Cache miss: modified file → re-analyzes
- Timestamp fast-path: unchanged mtime skips hashing
- Touch without content change: mtime differs but hash matches → cache hit
- Protocol version change → cache miss
close_reason: Already fully implemented: analysis_cache.rs with lookup/store/clear, mtime fast-path, SHA-256 hashing, protocol version validation, batch_analyze integration, CLI flags, and 9 passing tests

title: CLI: Skip unexecutable functions and report reasons

2. shatter-cli/src/main.rs in run_scan():

3. shatter-core/src/scan_orchestrator.rs:

title: Core: Executability check module

title: Core: TypeInfo::Opaque variant + JSON schema
description:
Add a new TypeInfo::Opaque { label: String } variant for runtime resource types that cannot be meaningfully constructed for testing (sockets, file descriptors, database connections, streams, channels). Distinct from Unknown (type couldn't be resolved) and Complex (type is known and constructible).

## What
1. shatter-core/src/types.rs — add Opaque { label: String } variant to TypeInfo enum. Add has_opaque(&self) -> bool recursive walker and find_opaque_label(&self) -> Option<String> helper that walk into Array elements, Object fields, Union variants, Nullable inner.
2. shatter-core/src/input_gen.rs — add Opaque { .. } arm to generate_random_value() returning Value::Null (caller should never reach this for opaque types, but safe fallback).
3. shatter-core/src/reporter.rs — add Opaque { label } arm to type display.
4. protocol/schemas/type-info.schema.json — add opaque kind with required label: string field.

## Why
Functions accepting runtime resources (net.Socket, *os.File, chan int) currently get random garbage inputs via Unknown, crash immediately, waste cycles, and produce noisy output. A distinct Opaque variant lets the system cleanly identify and skip these functions.

## Tests
- Round-trip serde for Opaque { label: "net.Socket" }
- Nested: Opaque inside Array, Object, Nullable
- has_opaque() returns true for direct and nested Opaque, false for all-primitive trees
- find_opaque_label() returns first label found in tree

title: BigInt/BigDecimal support end-to-end
description:
Full support for arbitrary-precision numeric types: generator, analyzer recognition, reconstruction, and tests.

## What
1. Core generators (input_gen.rs):
   - generate_bigint: 0, 1, -1, MAX_SAFE_INTEGER+1 (9007199254740992), MIN_SAFE_INTEGER-1, very large (50+ digits), powers of 2
   - generate_bigdecimal: 0, 0.01, -0.01, 0.1, 0.001, very precise (20+ decimal places), financial amounts
   - Wire format: {"__complex_type": "big_int", "value": "99999999999999999"}, {"__complex_type": "big_decimal", "value": "0.00000000000000001"}
   - String representation to avoid JSON number precision loss

2. TS analyzer: Recognize bigint primitive type (ts.TypeFlags.BigInt)
3. TS reconstruction: BigInt(value_string)
4. Go analyzer: Recognize *big.Int, *big.Float (as BigDecimal), *big.Rat
5. Go reconstruction: new(big.Int).SetString(value, 10), new(big.Float).SetString(value)

## Solver
Tier 1 for BigInt (if fits i64) — comparisons reduce to Z3 Int. Falls back to fuzzing for values exceeding i64 range.
Tier 3 for BigDecimal — fuzzing with good boundaries is sufficient.

## Export
- Jest: BigInt('digits')
- Go: new(big.Int).SetString("digits", 10)

## Integration test
safeDivide(a: bigint, b: bigint): bigint — division with zero-check branch

## Files
- shatter-core/src/input_gen.rs
- shatter-core/src/export.rs
- shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts
- shatter-ts/src/reconstruct.ts
- shatter-go/protocol/analyzer.go
- shatter-go/reconstruct/reconstruct.go
- examples/typescript/src/ (safeDivide test function)
close_reason: BigInt generator with boundary values (0, 1, -1, MAX_SAFE+1, large numbers) added. BigDecimal generator added. TS analyzer maps bigint primitive to complex:big_int. Go analyzer recognizes *big.Int, *big.Rat, *big.Float. Both frontends reconstruct BigInt values.

title: Error type support end-to-end
description:
Full support for Error/Exception types: generator, analyzer recognition, reconstruction, and tests.

## What
1. Core generator (input_gen.rs): generate_error with boundary-biased values
   - Boundaries: Error, TypeError, RangeError, SyntaxError, ReferenceError, custom class names; empty message, long message, message with special characters
   - Wire format: {"__complex_type": "error", "class": "TypeError", "message": "..."}
   - metadata carries {"class": "TypeError"} from the analyzer

2. TS analyzer: Recognize Error, TypeError, RangeError, SyntaxError, ReferenceError, EvalError, URIError by type symbol name. Store class name in metadata.
3. TS reconstruction: Switch on class name to construct appropriate Error subclass
4. Go analyzer: Recognize the error interface
5. Go reconstruction: errors.New(message) or fmt.Errorf(message)

## Solver
Tier 3 — fuzzing only. Error constraints are usually instanceof checks (already InstanceOf BinOp) or error.message string comparisons. The string comparison case works through existing solver if the frontend emits SymExpr::BinOp with the message field path.

## Export
- Jest: new TypeError('message')
- Go: errors.New("message")

## Files
- shatter-core/src/input_gen.rs
- shatter-core/src/export.rs
- shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts
- shatter-ts/src/reconstruct.ts
- shatter-go/protocol/analyzer.go
- shatter-go/reconstruct/reconstruct.go

   - Wire format: {"__complex_type": "url", "value": "https://..."}

3. TS reconstruction: new URL(value)
4. Go analyzer: Recognize *url.URL
5. Go reconstruction: url.Parse(value) (must handle error)

## Solver
Tier 2 — string equality/contains on the URL string works. URL component access (.protocol, .hostname, .pathname) would need the frontend to emit SymExpr::Call which falls back to fuzzing.

## Export

- Go: urlMustParse("url_string") (helper or inline)

title: RegExp support end-to-end
description:
Full support for regular expression types: generator, analyzer recognition, reconstruction, and tests.

## What
1. Core generator (input_gen.rs): generate_regexp with boundary-biased patterns
   - Boundaries: empty pattern, .*, \d+, [a-z], ^$, complex alternation (a|b|c), character classes, with flags (g, i, m, gi)
   - Wire format: {"__complex_type": "reg_exp", "source": "...", "flags": "..."}

2. TS analyzer: Recognize RegExp type
3. TS reconstruction: new RegExp(source, flags)
4. Go analyzer: Recognize *regexp.Regexp
5. Go reconstruction: regexp.MustCompile(source) (Go doesn't have regex flags in the same way — translate where possible)

## Solver
Tier 2 — partially solvable. Z3 has regex support (str.in.re). When a frontend instruments code like `if (pattern.test(input))`, the constraint can potentially be solved with Z3's string regex theory. Initially fall back to fuzzing; Z3 regex integration is a stretch goal.

## Export
- Jest: /source/flags
- Go: regexp.MustCompile("source")

## Files
- shatter-core/src/input_gen.rs
- shatter-core/src/export.rs
- shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts
- shatter-ts/src/reconstruct.ts
- shatter-go/protocol/analyzer.go
- shatter-go/reconstruct/reconstruct.go

title: Frontend protocol updates: TypeInfo::Complex in TS, Go, Rust
description:
Update all three frontend protocol type definitions to include the Complex variant and declare complex type capabilities in handshake.

## What
1. TypeScript (shatter-ts/src/protocol.ts or equivalent): Add complex variant to TypeInfo union type with ComplexKind enum
2. Go (shatter-go/protocol/types.go): Add ComplexKind and complex-related fields to TypeInfo struct
3. Rust frontend (shatter-rust/src/protocol.rs): Add complex variant to TypeInfo enum
4. Each frontend declares its supported complex types as complex_type:* entries in handshake capabilities

## Why
All frontends must understand TypeInfo::Complex to deserialize analysis results from the core and to serialize their own analysis output. The ComplexKind enum ensures type safety across the protocol boundary. Capability declarations let the core know which types each frontend can reconstruct.

## TS capabilities to declare

## Rust capabilities to declare

## Files

- shatter-go/protocol/types.go

SymExprConst extended with complex variant in TS. All frontend tests pass, clippy clean.

title: Core: Wire format output (concrete_to_json) and export formatting
description:
Update the orchestrator and test export to handle complex type wire format.

## What
1. Update concrete_to_json in orchestrator.rs to emit __complex_type tagged JSON for ConcreteValue::Complex
2. Update format_value (Jest) in export.rs to detect __complex_type in JSON objects and emit JS constructor calls
3. Update format_go_value in export.rs to detect __complex_type and emit Go constructor calls
4. Update go_type_from_value to handle __complex_type tagged objects

## Why
When the solver produces a ConcreteValue::Complex, the orchestrator must convert it to tagged JSON (e.g., {"__complex_type": "date", "value": 1704067200000}) for the protocol. When exporting tests, the export module must emit language-appropriate constructors (new Date(...) for Jest, time.UnixMilli(...) for Go) instead of raw JSON objects.

## Wire format
All complex values use a single __complex_type tag. The tag value matches ComplexKind's serde snake_case serialization. Additional fields are type-specific (value, source, flags, etc.).

## Export formatting
| Kind | Jest output | Go output |
|------|------------|-----------|
| date | new Date(epoch_ms) | time.UnixMilli(epoch_ms) |
| reg_exp | /source/flags | regexp.MustCompile("source") |
| big_int | BigInt('digits') | new(big.Int).SetString("digits", 10) |
| url | new URL('url') | urlMustParse("url") |
| uuid | 'uuid-string' | uuid.MustParse("uuid-string") |
| error | new TypeError('msg') | errors.New("msg") |

Initially implement detection framework + date; other kinds added as their generators are built.

## Files
- shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs — concrete_to_json
- shatter-core/src/export.rs — format_value, format_go_value, go_type_from_value

## Tests
- concrete_to_json produces correct tagged JSON for ConcreteValue::Complex
- format_value produces new Date(...) for date-tagged objects
- format_go_value produces time.UnixMilli(...) for date-tagged objects
- Unknown __complex_type falls through to existing JSON formatting
close_reason: concrete_to_json emits __complex_type tagged wire format for ConcreteValue::Complex. format_value handles date, regexp, bigint, url, error, uuid, symbol, option for Jest. format_go_value handles date, duration, regexp, bigint, url, error, ip_address, rune, go_byte for Go. go_type_from_value maps complex tags to Go types. 428 tests pass, clippy clean.

title: Core: FrontendCapabilities and capability-gated input generation
description:
Add LSP-style capability negotiation for complex types and gate input generation on declared capabilities.

## What
1. Add FrontendCapabilities struct that parses complex_type:* entries from the handshake capabilities Vec<String>
2. Update input_gen.rs to accept capabilities and check them before generating complex values
3. Fall back to generate_unknown() for complex types the frontend didn't declare

## Why
Different frontends support different complex types (TS has Symbol, Go has Rune, neither has the other's). The core must only generate types the frontend can reconstruct. Capability negotiation during handshake (like LSP's initialize) lets each frontend declare what it supports.

## Design
- FrontendCapabilities { commands: HashSet<String>, complex_types: HashSet<ComplexKind> }
- from_raw(capabilities: &[String]) parses "complex_type:date" → ComplexKind::Date
- supports_complex(kind: ComplexKind) -> bool
- generate_random_value gains a capabilities parameter (or a context struct)
- TypeInfo::Complex match arm checks caps.supports_complex(kind) before generating
- Handshake capability format: "complex_type:<serde_snake_case_name>" e.g. "complex_type:date", "complex_type:reg_exp", "complex_type:big_int"

## Files
- shatter-core/src/protocol.rs or new shatter-core/src/capabilities.rs — FrontendCapabilities
- shatter-core/src/input_gen.rs — capability-gated generation
- shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs — pass capabilities through exploration loop

## Tests
- FrontendCapabilities::from_raw correctly parses complex_type entries
- Unknown complex_type strings are silently ignored
- Non-complex capabilities ("analyze", "execute") parsed as commands
- generate_random_value falls back to Unknown for unsupported ComplexKind
- generate_random_value produces tagged JSON for supported ComplexKind
close_reason: FrontendCapabilities struct with from_raw() and supports_complex() added to orchestrator.rs. generate_random_value and generate_random_inputs gain caps parameter (Option<&FrontendCapabilities>). Complex type input gen checks capabilities before generating. 410 tests pass, clippy clean.

title: Core: ConstValue::Complex, ConcreteValue::Complex, and solver unwrap
description:
Extend the symbolic expression and constraint solver to handle complex type constants.

## What
1. Add ConstValue::Complex { kind: ComplexKind, repr: Box<ConstValue> } to shatter-core/src/sym_expr.rs
2. Add ConcreteValue::Complex { kind: ComplexKind, repr: Box<ConcreteValue> } to shatter-core/src/solver.rs
3. Handle ConstValue::Complex in the solver's to_z3_expr by unwrapping repr and recursing

## Why
When a frontend instruments code like `if (date > new Date("2024-01-01"))`, it needs to emit a constant that preserves the complex type identity while carrying an underlying primitive the solver can reason about. For dates, the repr is Int(epoch_millis); for bigint, Str(digit_string); for char, Int(codepoint). The solver unwraps repr and reasons about the primitive directly — no Z3 changes needed for Tier 1 solvable types.

## Design
- ConstValue::Complex { kind, repr } where repr is the underlying value Z3 reasons about
- ConcreteValue::Complex { kind, repr } for solved values that need to be tagged in the wire format
- Solver handles Complex by recursing: ConstValue::Complex { repr, .. } => to_z3_const(repr, hint_sort)

## Files
- shatter-core/src/sym_expr.rs — ConstValue::Complex variant
- shatter-core/src/solver.rs — ConcreteValue::Complex variant + to_z3_expr handling

## Tests
- Round-trip serialization for ConstValue::Complex with various repr types (Int, Str, Float)
- Round-trip serialization for ConcreteValue::Complex
- Solver correctly unwraps Complex(Date, Int(epoch)) in comparisons
- Solver returns Complex(Date, Int(solved_epoch)) in results
close_reason: ConstValue::Complex added to sym_expr.rs with kind+repr design. ConcreteValue::Complex added to solver.rs. Solver unwraps Complex repr for Z3 solving. concrete_to_json handles Complex. All 406 tests pass, clippy clean.

title: Core: ComplexKind enum and TypeInfo::Complex variant
description:
Add the foundational type system extension for complex (non-primitive, non-collection) types.

## What
Add a closed ComplexKind enum to shatter-core/src/types.rs with all well-known complex type variants (Date, DateTime, Time, Duration, RegExp, Char, Symbol, BigInt, BigDecimal, Complex, Rational, Range, Buffer, BitSet, Error, Option, Result, Closure, Iterator, Url, IpAddress, Uuid, Path, Money, SemVer, Email, MimeType, Color, GeoPoint, Locale, Rune, GoByte). Add a new TypeInfo::Complex { kind: ComplexKind, metadata: serde_json::Map, inner: Option<Box<TypeInfo>> } variant.

## Why
Shatter currently maps all non-primitive types to TypeInfo::Unknown, producing random garbage inputs that rarely exercise meaningful branches. A structured ComplexKind enum gives compile-time exhaustiveness checking — every match must handle all variants. The metadata field carries language-specific details (e.g., {"class": "TypeError"}). The inner field supports wrapper types (Option<T>, Result<T,E>).

## Design decisions
- Closed enum (not free-form string) for compile-time exhaustiveness checking
- Language-specific types (Rune, GoByte, Symbol) are regular enum variants, not a separate escape hatch — capability negotiation (separate issue) controls which get used
- serde(rename_all = "snake_case") for wire format consistency
- ComplexKind derives Copy + Hash + Eq for use in HashSets

## Files
- shatter-core/src/types.rs

## Tests
- Round-trip serialization for ComplexKind (all variants)
- Round-trip serialization for TypeInfo::Complex with metadata and inner
- TypeInfo::Complex with empty metadata and no inner
- Nested: TypeInfo::Complex inside TypeInfo::Array, TypeInfo::Object, etc.
close_reason: ComplexKind enum with 32 variants and TypeInfo::Complex variant added to types.rs. All match sites updated (input_gen, reporter, recursive). 20 type tests + 404 total tests pass. Clippy clean.
