title: Mirror source tree structure in .shatter/cache/ directory
description:
The current cache uses a flat namespace: `.shatter/cache/<sanitized_function_id>.json`. This will collide when two files contain functions with the same name (e.g. `src/auth.ts:validate` and `src/payments.ts:validate` both become `validate.json`).

## Proposed structure

Mirror the source tree, keeping file extensions to avoid collisions between e.g. `utils.ts` and `utils.js`:

```
.shatter/cache/
  src/
    auth.ts/                       # source file becomes directory (keep extension)
      validateToken.json           # free function
      TokenValidator/              # class becomes subdirectory
        validate.json              # method
        refresh.json
    payments/
      process.ts/
        calculateTotal.json
        getDiscount.json
```

## Mapping rules

- Source file path becomes a directory path, extension included
- Free functions are files directly inside the file directory
- Classes become subdirectories; methods are files inside them
- Target `src/auth.ts:validateToken` → `src/auth.ts/validateToken.json`
- Target `src/auth.ts:TokenValidator.validate` → `src/auth.ts/TokenValidator/validate.json`
- Nested classes (`Outer.Inner.method`) → `Outer/Inner/method.json`

## Implementation

- Update `BehaviorMapCache::path_for()` in `cache.rs` to split the function_id on `:` (file vs function) and `.` (class vs method) to build the directory path
- `sanitize_filename()` still needed for individual path components but no longer flattens the entire ID
- `load()` and `store()` unchanged in interface — only `path_for()` changes
- `store()` already does atomic write via temp file + rename; ensure the parent directory is created before writing

## Migration

- On first run after upgrade, old flat cache files are ignored (cache miss, function gets re-explored)
- No explicit migration needed — re-exploration is cheap and the old files are harmless
- Could optionally log a warning if flat files are detected and suggest clearing the cache

## Why keep the file extension

Stripping extensions would cause collisions: `utils.ts` and `utils.js` in the same directory would both map to `utils/`. All modern operating systems allow dots in directory names.
close_reason: Implementation complete: hierarchical path_for() mirrors source tree, sanitize_component() per-segment, parent dir creation in store(), comprehensive tests passing

title: Genetic/evolutionary input generation (optional, persisted)
description:
Add an optional genetic/evolutionary search mode for generating candidate inputs where the constraint solver fails (opaque predicates, hash comparisons, complex string matching, floating point).

## Motivation

Z3 cannot solve all constraints. When a branch condition involves a hash check, checksum validation, or other opaque computation, the solver returns unknown/unsat. Currently these branches remain uncovered. A genetic algorithm can hill-climb toward coverage of these branches using mutation, crossover, and coverage feedback — targeting specific unsolved partitions rather than blindly maximizing overall coverage (which is EvoSuite's weakness, generating many redundant inputs).

## Key design decisions

### Optional and explicit
- NOT part of the default exploration flow
- Activated via `--genetic` CLI flag or `genetic.enabled: true` in `.shatter/config.yaml`
- Expected to run occasionally (nightly, weekly, or manually) not on every commit
- Has its own iteration budget separate from the main exploration budget

### Persisted results
- Any test vector discovered by the genetic search that covers a new equivalence class gets persisted to the behavior map cache, exactly like solver-generated inputs
- Subsequent runs see these as cached behaviors — no need to re-run the genetic search
- The genetic search is a one-time cost whose value compounds across all future runs

### Targeted, not blind
- Only activated for equivalence classes where Z3 returned unknown/unsat
- Receives the unsolved path condition as a fitness hint (get closer to satisfying this)
- Uses coverage feedback from the frontend to measure progress
- Stops early if it finds a witness for the target partition

## Algorithm sketch
1. Seed population from existing concrete inputs (from prior exploration runs)
2. Mutate: type-aware perturbation (bit flips for ints, char substitution for strings, field shuffling for objects)
3. Crossover: combine fields from two parents
4. Evaluate: execute each candidate, measure branch distance to target
5. Select: tournament selection favoring candidates closer to the target branch
6. Repeat until witness found or budget exhausted
7. Persist any new behaviors discovered

## Configuration
- `genetic.enabled` (bool, default false) — master toggle
- `genetic.population_size` (u32, default 50) — candidates per generation
- `genetic.max_generations` (u32, default 100) — iteration budget
- `genetic.mutation_rate` (f64, default 0.3) — probability of mutating each field
- `genetic.crossover_rate` (f64, default 0.7) — probability of crossover vs clone
- `genetic.timeout` (u32, default 300) — total seconds budget

## Relation to other input strategies
Sits between dictionary values and LLM-guided generation in priority. Expensive but handles opaque constraints that cheaper strategies cannot.

## Prior art
- EvoSuite (Java): genetic algorithm for JUnit test generation
- AFL/libFuzzer: coverage-guided mutation on byte streams
- FUZZOLIC: hybrid concolic + fuzzing

title: Built-in dictionary of common boundary values by type
description:
Ship a pre-configured dictionary of values known to trigger boundary conditions and common bugs, organized by type. These supplement solver-generated and code-extracted candidates.

## Motivation
Many bugs cluster around well-known boundary values that are universal across codebases — epoch boundaries for dates, MAX_INT for integers, empty strings, null bytes, etc. A developer shouldn't need to rediscover these. The dictionary provides baseline coverage of classic edge cases without any code analysis.

## Example values by type

### Dates/timestamps
- 1969-12-31T23:59:59Z (one second before Unix epoch)
- 1970-01-01T00:00:00Z (Unix epoch)
- 2000-01-01, 2000-02-29 (Y2K, leap day)
- 2038-01-19T03:14:07Z (32-bit overflow)
- 9999-12-31 (max representable in many systems)

### Numbers (integers)
- 0, -1, 1
- MIN_INT, MAX_INT (for 8/16/32/64 bit)
- Powers of 2 and their neighbors (255/256/257, 65535/65536)
- NaN, Infinity, -Infinity (floats)
- 0.1 + 0.2 neighborhood (IEEE 754 precision)

### Strings
- empty string, single char, very long string
- null byte embedded ("\x00")
- Unicode edge cases (BOM, RTL marks, emoji, combining chars)
- SQL/XSS/path traversal payloads (for security-sensitive contexts)
- Strings that look like numbers ("0", "NaN", "null", "undefined")

### Collections
- empty, single element, two elements
- duplicate elements
- already sorted, reverse sorted

### Booleans and nulls
- true, false, null, undefined
- 0 and "" as falsy values (JS-specific)

## Implementation
- Core ships a built-in dictionary as a static data structure (not a file to load)
- Dictionary entries are tagged with type and category (boundary, security, unicode, etc.)
- Frontends can extend with language-specific values (e.g., JS falsy values, Go zero values)
- When the explorer encounters a parameter of a known type, it pulls relevant dictionary entries as initial candidates
- Dictionary values are lowest priority — solver-generated and code-extracted values take precedence

## Relation to other input sources
This is one of several candidate input strategies:
1. Constraint solver (Z3) — highest priority, mathematically precise
2. Code-extracted literals — from the codebase itself
3. Dictionary values — universal boundary conditions (this issue)
4. Genetic/evolutionary — fallback for opaque constraints
5. LLM-guided — semantic understanding
6. Random — last resort

title: Extract literals and constants from source code as candidate test inputs
description:
Simple static extraction pass: scan the target source code for literal values (string literals, numeric constants, named constants, enum values, default parameter values) and feed them into the candidate input pool.

This is a low-hanging-fruit input source that requires no symbolic execution or constraint solving — just AST/parse-level extraction. Many real-world branch conditions compare against magic strings, config constants, or enum values that appear elsewhere in the code. Extracting these and using them as candidate inputs dramatically increases the chance of covering those branches.

## Scope
- String literals
- Numeric constants (integers, floats)
- Named constants / const declarations
- Enum values and union type members
- Default parameter values
- Object keys used in property access / switch cases
- Regex patterns (for generating matching inputs)

## Implementation approach
- Each language frontend can implement this as a lightweight AST visitor
- Results sent back to core as a CandidateInputs protocol message
- Core merges these into the candidate pool alongside solver-generated and dictionary values
- No need for control flow analysis — pure extraction

## Prior art
This is analogous to what coverage-guided fuzzers call a 'dictionary' of tokens extracted from the binary (e.g., AFL's -x flag, libFuzzer's dict argument), but operating at the source level with type information preserved.
close_reason: Literal extraction expanded in TS and Go frontends, all tests pass

title: Staged pipeline: observe → analyze → solve → specify
description:
Refactor the exploration pipeline into independent, composable stages with typed interfaces and CLI subcommands.

## Design Decisions (resolved)

1. **Concolic loop**: Separate Observe and Solve. Observe executes from all input sources (seeds, fuzz, user-provided, Z3-solved). Solve/Generate produces new candidates from analysis state. This creates a clean seam for the future pluggable generator trait (str-4bx).

2. **Canonical IR**: Rename ExplorationResult → ObservationOutput as the single pipeline type. Delete the adapter layer and From conversions. Add Serialize/Deserialize for caching. Transition via type alias to limit blast radius.

3. **CLI exposure**: Three new subcommands — `shatter observe`, `shatter analyze`, `shatter specify`. Solve stays internal. Each reads/writes stage output as JSON for caching and composability.

4. **scan_orchestrator**: Updated only as a consequence of IR type changes, not a separate goal.

## Stages

- **Observe** (CLI: `shatter observe`): Run function with many inputs (random, boundary, user-provided, Z3-solved), record all execution data. No analysis or spec generation. Output: ObservationOutput (JSON-serializable).
- **Analyze** (CLI: `shatter analyze`): Takes ObservationOutput, produces AnalyzeOutput (equivalence classes, behavior map, coverage metrics). Pure function, no I/O.
- **Solve** (internal only): Takes AnalyzeOutput + uncovered branch constraints, produces new candidate inputs. Will become the pluggable generator trait in str-4bx.
- **Specify** (CLI: `shatter specify`): Takes ObservationOutput + AnalyzeOutput, produces FunctionSpec. Pure function.

## Scope

This is an architectural refactor — the pieces exist but are coupled. Key changes:
- Rename ExplorationResult → ObservationOutput, unify the two result types
- Deduplicate analyze() and analyze_observation() into one function on ObservationOutput
- Make build_spec() take pipeline types instead of ExplorationResult directly
- Restructure orchestrator::explore() to separate observation from solving
- Add Serialize/Deserialize to stage output types for caching
- Add three CLI subcommands that read/write stage JSON
- Update scan_orchestrator to use new types

## Not in scope
- Pluggable generator trait (str-4bx)
- Concurrent observers/generators (str-frc)
- Protocol changes (BatchExecute etc.)

title: Custom LLM adapter framework
description:
Provide an adapter framework for integrating Shatter with any LLM, including private cloud deployments and locally-hosted models.

## HTTP adapter (remote custom LLMs)
- Generic HTTP POST adapter configurable via config file
- Configurable endpoint URL (llm.custom.url), headers, auth scheme
- Configurable request/response JSON path mappings (where to place the prompt, where to read the response)
- Support for Bearer token, API key header, or no auth
- Timeout, retry, and backoff settings

## Local model adapter (host-resident LLMs)
- Support for OpenAI-compatible local servers (llama.cpp, ollama, vLLM, etc.) via the same HTTP adapter with localhost URLs
- Optional subprocess management: start/stop a local model server as a child process
  - llm.local.command — command to launch the server (e.g., 'ollama serve')
  - llm.local.model — model to load (e.g., 'llama3.2')
  - llm.local.port — port to connect on (default: 11434 for ollama)
  - llm.local.startup_timeout_seconds — time to wait for server readiness (default: 60)
  - Health check endpoint polling before sending requests

## Adapter registration
- Adapters register by name in config (llm.adapter = 'openai' | 'anthropic' | 'google' | 'custom' | 'local')
- Custom adapters selected via llm.adapter = 'custom' with llm.custom.* config block
- Local adapters selected via llm.adapter = 'local' with llm.local.* config block
- All adapters implement the same SeedOracle trait from the core LLM integration issue

title: LLM adapter: Google (Gemini)
description:
Implement a SeedOracle adapter for the Google Gemini API.

- Handle rate limiting with exponential backoff

- Handle API errors gracefully (auth failures, quota exceeded, model not found)

title: Optional LLM integration for seed generation and stuck-state recovery
description:
Add an optional LLM oracle that augments the concolic engine by generating candidate inputs where symbolic execution struggles. The LLM operates as a seed oracle in two phases: (1) cold-start seed generation before the concolic loop, and (2) stuck-state recovery when the solver fails to find new coverage for K rounds.

## Key design decisions

### Optional and explicit
- NOT part of the default exploration flow
- Activated via `--llm` CLI flag or `llm.enabled: true` in `.shatter/config.yaml`
- Expected to run occasionally (during initial exploration of a new codebase, or when stuck-state analysis is needed) not on every commit
- Has its own query budget separate from the main exploration budget

### Persisted results
- Any test vector discovered via LLM suggestion that covers a new equivalence class gets persisted to the behavior map cache, exactly like solver-generated inputs
- Subsequent runs see these as cached behaviors — no need to re-query the LLM
- The LLM cost is a one-time investment whose value compounds across all future runs
- Even if the LLM suggests 10 candidates and only 2 are useful, those 2 are permanently captured

## Where LLM adds value over Z3
- Opaque constraints (hashes, checksums, encrypted tokens)
- String/format validation (regex, email, URL, JSON structure)
- Domain-specific seeds (protocol messages, API payloads)
- Loop bound heuristics (suggest meaningful array lengths)
- Cold-start seed quality (structurally valid inputs from function signatures and names)

## Architecture
- New optional SeedOracle trait in shatter-core
- Pluggable backend: implementations provided by separate adapter crates/modules
- Invoked before concolic loop (cold-start) and between rounds (stuck-state)
- LLM receives: function source, parameter types, uncovered branch conditions, failed constraints
- LLM returns: candidate input JSON values, parsed and validated before use

## Recommended configuration parameters
- llm.enabled (bool) — master toggle, default false
- llm.cold_start_candidates (u32) — number of seed inputs to request at cold-start, default 5
- llm.stuck_candidates (u32) — number of inputs to request per stuck-state query, default 3
- llm.stuck_threshold (u32) — rounds with no new coverage before triggering stuck-state query, default 5
- llm.max_queries_per_function (u32) — budget cap to control cost, default 10
- llm.max_tokens_per_query (u32) — output token limit per LLM request, default 1024
- llm.temperature (f64) — sampling temperature, higher for more diverse candidates, default 0.7
- llm.timeout_seconds (u32) — per-request timeout, default 30
- llm.context_lines (u32) — lines of source context to send around the target function, default 50

## Output parsing and validation
- LLM output must be parsed as JSON array of candidate input objects
- Validate each candidate against the function's parameter types (reject type mismatches)
- Deduplicate against existing candidate pool and previously tested inputs
- Handle malformed LLM output gracefully: log warning, skip bad candidates, continue
- Retry with simplified prompt on parse failure (up to llm.max_retries, default 2)
- Structured output prompting preferred where the LLM API supports it (JSON mode, tool_use, etc.)

## Relation to other input strategies
Sits after genetic search in priority. Expensive but provides semantic understanding that no other strategy offers.

## Non-goals
- LLM does NOT replace the concolic engine or Z3 solver
- LLM is never in the hot path — only invoked at phase boundaries
- No LLM-based constraint solving (that is Z3's job)

title: Add .shatter/ configuration to examples as realistic project templates
description:
## Context

Depends on str-9js (candidate inputs and .shatter/ convention). Once the convention is implemented, the examples should demonstrate it.

## Problem

The current examples in `examples/typescript/` and `examples/go/` are flat collections of standalone functions. They don't resemble real projects — no package structure, no configuration, no candidate inputs. Users looking at the examples won't see how to set up .shatter/ for their own projects.

## What to Do

### Restructure examples as realistic mini-projects

Each example language directory should look like a small real project:

```
examples/
  typescript/
    package.json
    tsconfig.json
    .shatter/
      config.yaml               # project-level defaults + per-function overrides
      inputs/
        classifyNumber/
          candidates.json       # known edge cases for this function
        validateToken/
          candidates.json       # realistic auth payloads
        global/
          candidates.json       # common boundary values
    src/
      01-arithmetic.ts
      02-strings.ts
      ...
  go/
    go.mod
    .shatter/
      config.yaml
      inputs/
        ClassifyNumber/
          candidates.json
    ...
```

### Config examples should demonstrate

- Global defaults with reasonable values
- Per-function overrides showing both higher limits (complex functions) and lower limits (simple ones)
- `skip: true` for at least one function
- Candidate inputs that include: typical values, boundary values, regression cases (labeled)
- A glob pattern override (e.g., `"*_test.go":` skip)

### Candidate input examples should demonstrate

- Inputs matching each function's actual parameter types
- Labels explaining why each candidate matters ("boundary: zero", "regression: overflow", "production: typical payload")
- Both function-scoped and global candidates

## Acceptance Criteria

- Each example language has a `.shatter/config.yaml` and at least one `inputs/` directory
- `shatter run examples/typescript/` picks up the config and candidates automatically
- The examples serve as copy-paste templates for users setting up their own projects
- README or comments in config.yaml explain the convention for new users
close_reason: All acceptance criteria already met: all 3 example languages have .shatter/config.yaml, inputs/ directories, labeled candidates, global defaults, per-function overrides, skip directives, and glob patterns.

title: Explore bounded symbolic execution for array/collection parameters
description:
**This is a future exploration item, not a gap in current functionality.** The current engine already handles arrays through its general concolic loop — it executes concretely, collects per-element branch decisions, negates them with Z3, and falls back to fuzzing for unsolvable constraints. Execution budgets and plateau detection prevent runaway exploration. This works.

The question is whether we can do *better* with array-aware strategies. Sequential processing of collection elements causes exponential path explosion (2^n paths for n elements with one branch each), and the engine currently has no special knowledge that it's exploring array contents vs any other parameter. A smarter approach could focus exploration budget more effectively.

Areas to investigate:

1. **Bounded unrolling** — Recognize array parameters and deliberately generate inputs at lengths 0, 1, 2, 3 rather than random 0-5. Most real bugs appear at small sizes. Spend solving budget on per-element branches at those sizes.

2. **Early-exit path detection** — Identify find/some/indexOf patterns where interesting paths are linear (found at index k, not found) rather than exponential.

3. **Quantified constraints** — Evaluate whether Z3 forall/exists quantifiers are practical for simple array predicates like 'exists element > 0'.

4. **Loop summarization** — Research feasibility of inferring loop invariants for common patterns (filter, map, reduce with thresholds).

5. **Coverage-directed array mutation** — AFL-style splice/insert/delete mutations for array contents, complementing the existing fuzzing fallback.

Prerequisite: overlay_solved_values would need to reconstruct nested JSON from solved flat values for some of these approaches.

title: Support mutual recursion in compositional testing
description:
Extend recursive function support to handle mutually recursive function groups (e.g. is_even/is_odd, parser combinators).

## Context

Self-recursion (f calls f) is handled by iterative deepening with self-call mocking. Mutual recursion (f calls g, g calls f) requires bootstrapping the entire cycle simultaneously.

## Strategy

1. Detect strongly connected components (SCCs) in the call graph. Each SCC with >1 node is a mutual recursion group.
2. For each function in the group, identify base-case branches (branches that don't call any other function in the group).
3. Bootstrap: solve for base-case inputs across ALL functions in the group simultaneously, building initial BehaviorMaps for each.
4. Iterative deepening: at each depth, mock cross-calls within the group using BehaviorMaps from depth ≤ k. Explore all functions in the group at each depth.
5. The group's BehaviorMaps grow together. Stop when coverage stabilizes across all functions or max depth is reached.

## Implementation

1. Replace simple cycle detection in CallGraph with SCC detection (Tarjan's or Kosaraju's algorithm).
2. Single-node SCCs with a self-edge → self-recursive (handled by existing iterative deepening).
3. Multi-node SCCs → mutual recursion groups. Process as a unit in test_order().
4. Extend the orchestrator's deepening loop to handle groups: at each depth, explore each function in the group with mocks for all other functions in the group.

## Example: is_even(n) / is_odd(n)

- Base cases: is_even(0)→true, is_odd(0)→false
- Depth 1: is_even(1) calls is_odd(0)→false from mock → is_even(1)→false; is_odd(1) calls is_even(0)→true from mock → is_odd(1)→true
- Depth 2: is_even(2) calls is_odd(1)→true → is_even(2)→true; etc.

## Scope

This is lower priority than self-recursion support since mutual recursion is less common in practice. Most mutual recursion involves small cycles (2-3 functions). Large cycles may need special handling or manual annotation.

## Key files

- shatter-core/src/behavior.rs — CallGraph, test_order()
- shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs — explore loop

## Tests

- is_even/is_odd: mutual recursion detected, base cases found, BehaviorMaps built correctly
- Three-function cycle: A→B→C→A, all base cases found
- Mixed graph: some functions self-recursive, some mutually recursive, some neither — all handled correctly
close_reason: Implemented mutual recursion support: Tarjan's SCC algorithm in CallGraph, MutualGroup variant in TestOrderEntry, explore_mutual_group() for group-level iterative deepening.

title: Small-value probing for quick base-case discovery
description:
Add a lightweight heuristic that probes recursive functions with boundary values to quickly find base-case inputs before doing any symbolic analysis.

## Rationale

Base cases in recursive functions almost always guard against 'empty' or 'minimal' inputs. Probing with a small set of canonical values is cheap and hits base cases in the vast majority of real recursive functions.

## Implementation

1. Define a set of canonical probe values per type: numbers {0, 1, -1}, strings {"", "a"}, arrays {[], [x]}, booleans {false, true}, null/undefined.
2. When a function is identified as self-recursive, before symbolic solving, run it with probe values (combinatorially for multi-arg functions, but capped at a small budget like 20 executions).
3. Executions that terminate without a recursive call are base-case witnesses. Record them as the initial BehaviorMap entries.
4. This runs as an optional fast pass before the constraint-solving path. If it finds base cases, downstream iterative deepening can start immediately.

## Heuristic: Argument-size monotonicity

As a refinement, detect which argument decreases in the recursive call (n-1, arr.slice(1), node.left, etc.). If detectable, pick probe values that are 'small' in that dimension specifically. This further increases hit rate.

## Key files

- shatter-core/src/explorer.rs — RandomExplorer or new ProbeExplorer
- shatter-core/src/behavior.rs — BehaviorMap::from_records()

## Tests

- Probing factorial(n) with {0, 1, -1} finds base cases n=0 and n=1
- Probing fibonacci(n) with small values finds the base cases
- Probing a function with array argument using [] finds the empty-list base case
- Multi-argument recursive function: probing stays within execution budget
close_reason: Implemented probe_values() and generate_probe_inputs() for small-value probing. Covers canonical boundary values per type (0/1/-1 for ints, empty for strings/arrays, null for nullable). Combinatorial inputs capped at configurable budget.

Support --resume flag to continue an interrupted scan from where it left off. Cache invalidation: if a function's dependencies changed, re-explore even if its own source didn't change.

title: Go native fuzz tests for shatter-go parsing and deserialization
description:
Add Go 1.18+ native fuzzing (testing.F) to shatter-go for all input parsing and deserialization code. Distinct from rapid property tests — native fuzzing finds panics and crashes on malformed input, rapid tests logical properties.

Targets:
- Protocol message parsing in handler.go
- Env var parsing (SHATTER_EXEC_TIMEOUT, etc.)
- Any JSON deserialization paths

Seed corpus from existing test cases. Run with 'go test -fuzz=FuzzXxx' for continuous fuzzing, or short durations in CI.

title: Add Kani bounded model checking to shatter-core solver bridge
description:
Add Kani proof harnesses for the most critical solver bridge invariants. Deferred — contracts (str-xlo9) and proptest (str-ho1d) provide most of the value at lower cost. Kani adds mathematical proof for ALL inputs, justified only for the highest-stakes properties.

## Targets (when justified)

- infer_sort(): prove sort matches TypeInfo for all SymExpr shapes and param types
- overlay_solved_values(): prove output length equals input length and types preserved for all inputs
- WorklistEntry Ord: prove total ordering (becomes non-trivial after fitness-based ordering from str-oi1d)
- Constraint negation: prove negated constraint is the logical complement

## Prerequisites

- str-xlo9 (contracts) — write contracts first, then prove them with Kani
- str-ho1d (proptest) — proptest covers the same properties probabilistically; Kani upgrades from 'very likely correct' to 'proven correct'

## Overhead

- ~500MB toolchain install (cargo install kani-verifier && cargo kani setup)
- Custom rustc backend, separate from cargo test
- Cannot cross Z3 FFI — verifies Rust-side logic only (sort inference, overlay, constraint construction)
- Each harness: 10-30s for simple, minutes-hours for complex unwind bounds
- Separate CI job, not part of standard cargo test

## Relationship with contracts

Complementary. Contracts document and check at runtime. Kani proves universally. A Kani proof subsumes the runtime check mathematically, but the contract remains valuable as inline documentation and as a safety net for FFI-dependent paths Kani can't reach.

title: VS Code extension for Shatter
description: Design and implement a VS Code extension that surfaces concolic exploration results directly in the editor: branch coverage gutters, CodeLens explore triggers, inline branch annotations with triggering inputs, diagnostics integration, behavioral spec hovers, and generated test preview. Requires new core outputs: coverage index JSON, exploration event stream, fingerprint manifest. Plan: docs/plans/vscode-extension-design.md

title: Time-travel analysis: --since with end boundary before HEAD
description: Support analyzing code as it existed at a historical range, not just identifying changed files. This combines --since <start-ref> with an end boundary (e.g. --until <end-ref>) where the end ref is before HEAD. Requires checking out file contents at the end ref, which creates .shatter data mismatch issues (seeds, specs, pool correspond to HEAD, not the historical version). Design considerations: temporary checkout of file contents, isolated .shatter state, clear UX for what 'analysis results' mean when not analyzing current code. Deferred until the basic --changed/--since feature is proven.

title: Core telemetry module (shatter-core/src/telemetry.rs)
description: Create shatter-core/src/telemetry.rs with: TelemetryEvent struct (envelope) and EventPayload enum (bad_cli_args, command_run, command_error). is_enabled() — checks consent hierarchy (SHATTER_TELEMETRY env → DO_NOT_TRACK env → ~/.config/shatter/telemetry.yaml → default enabled). generate_anonymous_id() — SHA-256(hostname + OS + arch + random salt), persisted to config. sanitize_args() — 7-rule token classifier: (1) split --flag=value, (2) preserve known subcommands, (3) preserve flag names, (4) replace paths with <path>.ext + probe filesystem metadata (exists/kind/parent_exists/parent_writable/depth), (5) preserve numbers, (6) preserve known enum values, (7) scrub everything else to <value>. queue_event() — append to XDG_DATA_HOME/shatter/telemetry/events.jsonl with fs2 file locking + 1MB size cap. read_config()/write_config() — YAML at XDG config path. show_first_run_notice() — one-time stderr message. Constants: TELEMETRY_SCHEMA_VERSION, TELEMETRY_MAX_QUEUE_BYTES (1MB), TELEMETRY_MAX_AGE_DAYS (30), TELEMETRY_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS (10). Add pub mod telemetry to lib.rs. New deps: uuid v1 (v4 feature), dirs crate. Existing deps: sha2, rand, fs2, serde, serde_json, serde_yaml. Tests: consent hierarchy, all 7 sanitization rules + path enrichment, queue append/size-cap/locking, event serialization round-trip, anonymous ID stability/uniqueness.

title: Containerized whole-tool execution
description:
Run the entire Shatter tool (core + frontends) inside a container or microVM for isolation. The target project directory on the host would be bind-mounted (containers) or virtiofs-mounted (microVMs) into the execution environment, avoiding checkout/build steps.

Motivation:
- Shatter executes arbitrary user code with adversarially-generated inputs — textbook sandboxing use case
- Whole-tool isolation is simpler than per-subprocess: one boundary, one mount, one lifecycle
- Standard UX: docker run -v $(pwd):/work shatter explore /work/src/foo.ts
- Also solves distribution friction (eliminates multi-toolchain install)

Output handling:
- Default: read-write bind mount of project directory — Shatter writes .shatter/ cache and generated tests directly, visible on host immediately
- Optional split mount for defense-in-depth: source read-only, output paths (.shatter/, generated-tests/) read-write
- Container must not require users to pre-create output directories — entrypoint should mkdir -p as needed

Implementation considerations:
- Start with OCI container (Dockerfile) — ubiquitous tooling, bind mounts are native speed
- MicroVMs (Firecracker, Cloud Hypervisor) worth considering if Shatter is ever offered as a hosted service
- Container image needs: Rust runtime, Node.js 22+, Go 1.24+, Z3 libs
- Mount target project read-only where possible; output directory read-write
- Preserve stdio streaming for progress output

title: Deferred: Z3 string theory for split() and regex
description: Z3 has no direct equivalent for string split operations — would need bounded unrolling to a configurable max segment count. Z3 supports str.in_re for regex but JS/Go/Rust regex features (backreferences, lookahead, named groups) exceed Z3's decidable regex fragment. These are real limitations but affect fewer functions than the core string operations (indexOf, contains, startsWith, endsWith, length, substr). Defer until core string solving is proven out. Workaround: frontend-side structural candidate generation for split/regex-heavy functions.

title: Call-graph-aware input propagation for cross-function seeding
description: When function A calls function B, and B's exploration found that input x triggers an error/rare path, use backward constraint solving to find inputs to A that cause A to pass x to B. This extends the interesting input pool (type-keyed) with targeted propagation: add 'reach callee B with argument x' as a target constraint during A's concolic exploration. The constraint solver already does forward path exploration, so this is a natural extension. Depends on the basic type-keyed input pool being implemented first. Key design questions: (1) how to express callee-argument-value targets in the existing SymConstraint system, (2) budget allocation between normal exploration and targeted propagation, (3) whether to limit to direct callers or propagate transitively through the call graph.

title: Functional types end-to-end (Closure, Iterator)
description:
Support for functional types that can be passed as inputs: generators, reconstruction, and tests.

## What
1. Core generators (input_gen.rs):
   - generate_closure: Small set of canned function variants that frontends know how to construct:
     - "identity": returns its first argument
     - "constant": always returns a fixed value (include the value in wire format)
     - "thrower": always throws an error
     - "noop": returns undefined/nil
     Wire: {"__complex_type": "closure", "variant": "identity"} or {"__complex_type": "closure", "variant": "constant", "value": 42}

   - generate_iterator: Generate a small array of values and let the frontend wrap in its iterator protocol
     Wire: {"__complex_type": "iterator", "values": [1, 2, 3]}

2. TS reconstruction:
   - closure: switch on variant to return appropriate function: (x) => x, () => value, () => { throw new Error() }, () => {}
   - iterator: values[Symbol.iterator]() or generator function

3. Go reconstruction:
   - closure: Generate Go function values (func(x interface{}) interface{} { return x })
   - iterator: Not natively applicable in Go (no iterator protocol). Skip or use channel/slice.

## Solver
Tier 3 — not representable symbolically. Fuzzing with the canned variants provides enough coverage to test callback-taking functions.

## Note
This is lower priority because callback-heavy code is harder to test concretely. The main value is being able to explore higher-order functions at all rather than failing on Unknown type.

## Files
- shatter-core/src/input_gen.rs
- shatter-ts/src/reconstruct.ts
- shatter-go/reconstruct/reconstruct.go
close_reason: All generators implemented in input_gen.rs: Option/Result (with present/ok flags), Complex/Rational/Range numerics, Closure (canned variants), Iterator (value arrays), and all domain-specific types (Money, SemVer, Email, Color, GeoPoint, Locale, MimeType). Full dispatch, export formatting, and reconstruction in both frontends.

title: Extended numeric types end-to-end (Complex, Rational, Range)
description:
Full support for complex numbers, rational numbers, and ranges: generators, analyzer recognition, and tests.

## What
1. Core generators (input_gen.rs):
   - generate_complex_number: (0,0), (1,0) purely real, (0,1) purely imaginary, (-1,-1), large values, (3,4) Pythagorean
     Wire: {"__complex_type": "complex", "real": 3.0, "imag": 4.0}
   - generate_rational: 0/1, 1/1, 1/2, -1/3, 22/7, large numerator/denominator, improper fractions
     Wire: {"__complex_type": "rational", "num": 22, "den": 7}
   - generate_range: empty (5..5), single (5..6), 0..10, -10..10, large, reversed (10..0), inclusive vs exclusive
     Wire: {"__complex_type": "range", "start": 0, "end": 10, "inclusive": false}

2. Go analyzer: Recognize *big.Rat as Rational
3. Go reconstruction: new(big.Rat).SetFrac64(num, den)
4. These types are less common in TS — no TS recognition needed initially

## Solver
- Complex: Tier 3 — fuzzing only (complex arithmetic is rare in branch conditions)
- Rational: Tier 1 — can be represented as Z3 Real (num/den ratio)
- Range: Tier 1 — start/end are integers, comparisons reduce to integer comparisons

## Files
- shatter-core/src/input_gen.rs
- shatter-go/protocol/analyzer.go (for big.Rat)
- shatter-go/reconstruct/reconstruct.go
